questions.q: Reverse a string WITHOUT using a built-in Reverse(). Implement it yourself.

answer.oneLiner: The approach is to convert the string to a character array and use two pointers starting at opposite ends, swapping characters and moving inward until they meet in the middle. This works because each swap correctly repositions two characters at once, so we only need to touch half the array. It runs in O(n) time and O(n) space because C# strings are immutable and we need the intermediate array.
keyPoints: Because strings are immutable in C#, you first copy the characters into a char array so you can swap in place.
Place one pointer called left at index zero and another called right at the last index.
On each iteration swap the characters at left and right, then move left forward and right backward.
Stop when the two pointers meet or cross, at which point every character has been repositioned.

code.note: O(n) time, O(n) space for the char array

dialogue.text: Reverse a string, but don't call any built-in Reverse. How would you approach it?
Since C# strings are immutable, I'd copy to a `char[]`, then use two pointers, one at each end, swapping inward until they cross.
Walk me through the complexity.
It's O(n) time since each character is touched once, and O(n) space for the char array; if I could mutate a char[] directly it'd be O(1) extra.
Any edge cases or inputs that break a naive swap?
Empty or single-char strings are fine since the loop just doesn't run; the real trap is Unicode surrogate pairs, where blind char swapping corrupts the codepoint.

followUpsQA.q: How do you make it O(1) extra space?
followUpsQA.a: Mutate a char[] in place rather than allocating a new one.

followUpsQA.q: How would you reverse only the words, not the characters?
followUpsQA.a: Split on whitespace, reverse the word array, then join.

followUpsQA.q: How do you handle Unicode surrogate pairs?
followUpsQA.a: Reverse by text element (StringInfo) so combined codepoints stay intact.

questions.q: Given an integer array of size n, find all elements that appear at least ⌊n/3⌋ times. Example: {3,2,3,1,1,1,2,2,3} → [1,2,3]. Constraints: 1 <= n <= 5*10^4, -10^9 <= nums[i] <= 10^9.

answer.oneLiner: The straightforward approach is to count every value with a hash map in one pass and then return all entries whose count meets or exceeds the floor of n divided by 3. This is O(n) time and O(n) space. The Boyer-Moore majority vote trick does not apply here because that algorithm is designed for elements appearing strictly more than n divided by 3 times, which guarantees at most two winners, whereas the inclusive threshold in this problem can produce three or more qualifying values.
keyPoints: The threshold is the floor of n divided by 3 and the comparison is greater than or equal to, so a value that appears exactly that many times does qualify.
Up to three distinct values can each appear exactly n divided by 3 times and together account for the whole array, which means a fixed two-candidate voting scheme will miss valid answers.
A hash map that maps each element to its count handles any number of winners correctly in O(n) time and O(n) space.
If the caller requires a deterministic ordering of the output, sort the result list before returning it.

code.note: O(n) time, O(n) space — hash map, not Boyer-Moore

dialogue.text: Find all elements appearing at least ⌊n/3⌋ times. For {3,2,3,1,1,1,2,2,3} the answer is [1,2,3].
Since each of 1, 2, 3 appears exactly 3 times and ⌊9/3⌋ = 3, an inclusive >= test keeps all three. I'd count with a hash map and return every key whose count is at least the threshold.
Could you use Boyer-Moore voting to get O(1) space like the classic n/3 problem?

No — that trick relies on at most two elements being able to exceed n/3. With 'at least', up to three values can each hit exactly n/3 (3 × n/3 = n), so a two-candidate scheme would miss one. The hash-map count is the safe approach.
Complexity and edge cases?
O(n) time and O(n) space. Watch the boundary: for small n, ⌊n/3⌋ can be 0, in which case every distinct value qualifies — worth confirming with the interviewer.

followUpsQA.q: Why can't Boyer-Moore voting solve the 'at least n/3' variant?
followUpsQA.a: Voting guarantees ≤2 candidates above n/3, but 'at least' (inclusive) allows up to 3 values at exactly n/3, so a fixed 2-slot scheme is insufficient.

followUpsQA.q: What's the answer under the strict 'more than ⌊n/3⌋' rule instead?
followUpsQA.a: For this balanced array it's empty, since no value exceeds 3; that version has at most 2 answers.

followUpsQA.q: How do you keep it simple and correct?
followUpsQA.a: A Dictionary of counts is O(n) space but trivially handles any number of winners.

questions.q: Count how many times each element occurs in an array using a Dictionary.

answer.oneLiner: You iterate through the array exactly once and for each element either insert it into a Dictionary with a count of one or increment its existing count, giving you a complete frequency table at the end. This runs in O(n) time and O(k) space where k is the number of distinct values, because each element causes at most one lookup and one write.
keyPoints: The Dictionary maps each unique element to the number of times it has appeared so far.
You can use GetValueOrDefault to safely read a count that might not exist yet and then write back the incremented value in one line.
Each individual lookup and insert is O(1) on average due to hashing, so the full pass stays linear.
After the loop the Dictionary contains every distinct value paired with its exact frequency.

code.note: O(n) time, O(k) space

dialogue.text: Count how many times each element occurs in an array.
I'd iterate once and accumulate into a Dictionary keyed by the element with the running count as the value.
What's the time and space cost?
O(n) time for the single pass with average O(1) lookups, and O(k) space where k is the number of distinct values.
How do you increment safely on the first sighting of a key?
I use `TryGetValue(x, out int c); dict[x] = c + 1;` so a missing key defaults c to 0, no separate ContainsKey check needed.

followUpsQA.a: nums.GroupBy(x => x).ToDictionary(g => g.Key, g => g.Count()).

followUpsQA.q: How do you get the most frequent element from this?
followUpsQA.a: Take the entry with the max value (e.g., OrderByDescending or a single max scan).

followUpsQA.q: How do you find duplicates from these counts?
followUpsQA.a: Keep keys whose count is greater than 1.

questions.q: Find all duplicate elements in an array.

answer.oneLiner: Walk the array once with a HashSet that tracks every value you have seen so far; if adding an element returns false then that element has appeared before and is a duplicate. Using a second HashSet for the results ensures each duplicate value is reported only once even if it appears three or more times. The whole algorithm is O(n) time and O(n) space.
keyPoints: HashSet.Add returns false when the value is already present, which is a clean way to detect repetition without extra lookups.
Keep a separate results set so that a value appearing three or more times is only added to the output once.
The algorithm completes in a single pass through the array, and each add or membership check is O(1) on average.
At the end convert the results set to whatever collection type the caller expects.

code.note: O(n) time, O(n) space

dialogue.text: Find all duplicate elements in an array.
I'd walk the array with a HashSet of seen values; `Add` returns false when the value already exists, which flags a duplicate.
What stops you from reporting the same duplicate twice?
I push flagged values into a second result set, so an element appearing three times still reports once.
Complexity, and can you beat the space?
O(n) time, O(n) space. If the values are bounded in [1..n], I can do O(1) extra space by negating the value at index x as a visited marker.

followUpsQA.q: Can you do it in O(1) extra space?
followUpsQA.a: If values are in [1..n], mark visited by negating nums[abs(x)-1].

followUpsQA.q: How would you return the count of each duplicate?
followUpsQA.a: Use a Dictionary of counts and keep entries with count > 1.
HashSet is O(n); sorting to find adjacent dups is O(n log n).

questions.q: Find the second largest element in an array in a single pass.

answer.oneLiner: You maintain two variables, one for the largest value seen so far and one for the second largest, updating both carefully as you scan the array from left to right in a single pass. This works because every element only needs to be compared against those two variables, so the problem reduces to bookkeeping rather than sorting. It runs in O(n) time and uses O(1) extra space.
keyPoints: Initialize both the largest and second largest variables to long.MinValue so that even arrays containing int.MinValue are handled correctly.
When you encounter a value greater than the current largest, demote the current largest to second largest and promote the new value to largest.
When a value is not greater than the largest but is greater than the current second largest and is not equal to the largest, update only the second largest.
After the loop check whether the second largest is still long.MinValue, which signals that the array had fewer than two distinct values and no valid answer exists.

code.note: O(n) time, O(1) space

dialogue.text: Find the second largest element in a single pass.
I'd track `first` and `second` as I scan: if x beats first, second becomes the old first and first becomes x; else if x is between, update second.
How do you avoid duplicates of the max becoming the second largest?
In the else branch I require `x > second && x != first`, so a repeated max won't slide into second.
Edge cases and complexity?
O(n) time, O(1) space. I init both to long.MinValue to survive int.MinValue inputs, and return null if no valid second exists.

followUpsQA.q: Should duplicates of the max count as second largest?
followUpsQA.a: Usually no; guard with x != first so {5,5,3} returns 3.

followUpsQA.q: How do you generalize to the k-th largest?
followUpsQA.a: Use a size-k min-heap or QuickSelect for O(n) average.

followUpsQA.q: What if the array has fewer than two distinct values?
followUpsQA.a: Return null since no second largest exists.

questions.q: Merge two sorted arrays into one sorted array.

answer.oneLiner: Place one pointer at the start of each sorted array and repeatedly pick whichever front element is smaller, copying it into a result array and advancing that pointer forward. Once one array is exhausted you copy the remaining elements of the other directly, since they are already sorted. This runs in O(m plus n) time and O(m plus n) space and is exactly the merge step at the heart of merge sort.
keyPoints: This is the same merge subroutine used internally by merge sort, so understanding it well gives insight into that algorithm too.
On each step compare the element at the current position in the first array against the element at the current position in the second array and copy the smaller one into the output.
Advance only the pointer belonging to the array you just copied from, leaving the other pointer in place.
When one array runs out of elements, append all remaining elements from the other array without any further comparisons because they are already in order.

code.note: O(m+n) time, O(m+n) space; stable

dialogue.text: Merge two sorted arrays into one sorted array.
This is the merge step of merge sort: one pointer per array, repeatedly take the smaller front element into the result.
What happens when one array runs out before the other?
After the main loop, I drain whatever remains in either array with two cleanup loops, since the leftover is already sorted.
Complexity, and can you do it in place?
O(m+n) time and O(m+n) space here. If one array has trailing capacity, I can merge in place by filling from the back to avoid overwriting unread values.

followUpsQA.q: How do you merge in place with trailing capacity?
followUpsQA.a: Fill from the back, comparing the two tails so you never overwrite unread data.

followUpsQA.q: How does this relate to merge sort's O(n log n)?
followUpsQA.a: Each level merges in O(n); there are log n levels.
Yes, taking from the first array on ties preserves order.

questions.q: Given a sentence, reverse the order of the words and count how many times each word occurs.

answer.oneLiner: Split the input on whitespace to get individual words, reverse the order of those words to form the new sentence, and simultaneously tally how many times each word appears using a Dictionary. Everything happens in linear time relative to the number of characters and the space used is proportional to the number of distinct words.
keyPoints: Use Split with the RemoveEmptyEntries option so that multiple consecutive spaces do not produce empty tokens in the word array.
Reverse the word array in place or iterate from the last index down to zero, then join the words back into a string with a single space separator.
Increment the count in a Dictionary for each word to build the frequency table in the same pass or a second pass over the word array.
If the comparison should be case-insensitive, pass a case-insensitive string comparer when constructing the Dictionary so that words differing only in capitalisation are treated as identical.

code.note: O(n) time over characters, O(w) space for word map

dialogue.text: Given a sentence, reverse the order of words and count how many times each word occurs.
I'd split on whitespace with RemoveEmptyEntries, tally each word in a Dictionary, then reverse the word array and join it back.
How do you handle messy spacing or case sensitivity?
RemoveEmptyEntries collapses runs of spaces, and I use a case-insensitive comparer (OrdinalIgnoreCase) if 'The' and 'the' should count as one.
What's the complexity?
O(n) over the characters for splitting and counting, and O(w) space for the word map where w is the number of words.

followUpsQA.q: How do you reverse the words without Array.Reverse?
followUpsQA.a: Two-pointer swap from both ends of the word array.

followUpsQA.q: How would you also reverse characters within each word?
followUpsQA.a: Reverse each word's char[] before joining.

followUpsQA.q: How do you return the most frequent word?
followUpsQA.a: Take the dictionary entry with the maximum count.

questions.q: Generate the first n numbers of the Fibonacci series iteratively.

answer.oneLiner: The iterative approach keeps track of just the previous two numbers and adds them together in a loop to produce the next one, which is why it only needs constant extra memory regardless of how many numbers you generate. You avoid the massive redundant recalculation that kills naive recursion, bringing the time cost down to a single linear pass. If you store the full series the space cost grows with n, but the computation itself stays O(n) time.
keyPoints: Iterative solution avoids the exponential blowup of naive recursion, which recalculates the same subproblems over and over.
You only need to track two variables at a time, updating them each iteration so the new value becomes the next starting point.
Use a long integer type rather than int to push back the point at which the values overflow for large inputs.

code.note: O(n) time, O(1) extra space

dialogue.text: Generate the first n Fibonacci numbers iteratively.
I'd keep two rolling variables a and b and update them as (a, b) = (b, a + b) in a loop, yielding a each step.
Why iterative rather than recursive?
Naive recursion recomputes overlapping subproblems and is O(2^n); the iterative roll is O(n) time and O(1) space.
Any practical concern with large n?
Values grow fast, so I use `long` to delay overflow; for very large n I'd switch to BigInteger.

followUpsQA.a: It recomputes the same subproblems; memoization collapses it to O(n).

followUpsQA.q: How do you compute the n-th Fibonacci in O(log n)?
followUpsQA.a: Matrix exponentiation or fast doubling.

followUpsQA.q: How do you handle overflow for large n?
followUpsQA.a: Use BigInteger instead of long.

questions.q: Print all prime numbers up to n efficiently.

answer.oneLiner: The Sieve of Eratosthenes works by assuming every number is prime to start, then systematically crossing off multiples of each prime it finds, which eliminates composites far more efficiently than testing each number individually. Starting the crossing-off at p squared rather than 2p is the key optimization because every smaller multiple was already eliminated by an earlier prime. This gives you all primes up to n in O(n log log n) time using a boolean array of size n.
keyPoints: You maintain a boolean array where each index represents a number and the value indicates whether it is still considered prime.
For each prime p you find, you start crossing out its multiples from p squared onward because any smaller multiple already has a smaller prime factor and was crossed out earlier.
You only need to iterate p up to the square root of n, because any composite number larger than that must have a factor smaller than or equal to the square root.

code.note: O(n log log n) time, O(n) space

dialogue.text: Print all prime numbers up to n efficiently.
Brute force is trial division per number, O(n*sqrt(n)). Better is the Sieve of Eratosthenes: mark multiples of each prime as composite.
Any optimization on where you start crossing out?
I start at p*p, because smaller multiples of p were already crossed by smaller primes, and I only loop p up to sqrt(n).
Complexity and memory?
O(n log log n) time and O(n) space for the boolean array; for huge ranges a segmented sieve bounds memory.

followUpsQA.q: How do you check a single number for primality?
followUpsQA.a: Trial division up to sqrt(n), O(sqrt n).

followUpsQA.q: Why start crossing out at p*p?
followUpsQA.a: Smaller multiples of p were already marked by smaller primes.

followUpsQA.q: How do you sieve a very large range?
followUpsQA.a: Segmented sieve processes blocks to cap memory.

questions.q: Count the number of trailing zeros in n! (factorial of n).

answer.oneLiner: Every trailing zero in a factorial comes from multiplying a 2 and a 5 together to make 10, and since factors of 2 always outnumber factors of 5 in any factorial, counting the fives is all you need. You count how many times 5 divides into the numbers from 1 to n by summing the integer divisions n divided by 5, then n divided by 25, and so on, because multiples of 25 contribute an extra factor of 5 beyond what the first pass catches. The whole calculation runs in O(log n) time and uses no extra space.
keyPoints: You should never try to compute the factorial itself because it overflows even 64-bit integers extremely quickly for moderate values of n.
Each multiple of 5 in the range contributes one factor of 5, and each multiple of 25 contributes an additional factor on top of that, which is why you need to account for higher powers separately.
You keep summing the floor of n divided by successive powers of 5 until the term reaches zero, and that total is the number of trailing zeros.

code.note: O(log_5 n) time, O(1) space

dialogue.text: Count the number of trailing zeros in n factorial.
A trailing zero comes from a factor of 10, which is 2*5. Since 5s are rarer than 2s, I just count factors of 5.
How do you count all the 5s correctly?
Sum floor(n/5) + floor(n/25) + floor(n/125)..., because multiples of 25 contribute an extra 5, multiples of 125 yet another, and so on.
Why not just compute n! and count zeros?
n! overflows almost immediately; my approach is O(log n) time, O(1) space and never builds the factorial.

followUpsQA.a: Factors of 2 are far more abundant, so 5s are the limiting factor.

followUpsQA.q: How do you count trailing zeros in base b?
followUpsQA.a: Factor b into primes and take the min over each prime's count in n!.
It overflows fast; the divisor-sum trick avoids it entirely.

questions.q: What sorting algorithm is preferred for linked lists, and why?

answer.oneLiner: Merge sort is the natural fit for linked lists because it only ever needs to walk nodes sequentially and relink pointers, never jumping to an arbitrary index the way quicksort or heapsort do. It guarantees O(n log n) in all cases with no worst-case degradation, and a bottom-up iterative version avoids even the recursion stack, keeping auxiliary space at O(1). The merge step is also stable, preserving the relative order of equal elements.
keyPoints: Quicksort and heapsort both rely on fast random access by index, which linked lists cannot provide in constant time, making those algorithms a poor match.
Merge sort only needs to traverse nodes in order and update next and previous pointers during the merge step, which is exactly what linked lists do efficiently.
Merge sort is stable, meaning elements with equal keys stay in their original relative order, which is a useful guarantee that quicksort does not provide.
A bottom-up iterative merge sort eliminates the recursion call stack entirely, so auxiliary space stays at O(1) rather than O(log n).

code.note: O(n log n) time; relinks nodes, no random access

dialogue.text: What sorting algorithm is preferred for linked lists, and why?
Merge sort. It only needs sequential access and merges by relinking nodes, so it fits a list far better than algorithms that need indexing.
Why is quicksort a poor fit here?
Quicksort and heapsort rely on O(1) random access for partitioning or heap indexing, which a singly linked list can't give cheaply.
What about the recursion stack space?
Top-down merge sort uses O(log n) stack, but bottom-up merge sort iterates over run sizes and reaches O(1) auxiliary space, with a guaranteed O(n log n).

followUpsQA.q: Why is quicksort poor for linked lists?
followUpsQA.a: Partitioning needs random access, which lists lack.

followUpsQA.q: How does bottom-up merge sort reach O(1) extra space?
followUpsQA.a: It iterates run sizes and splices pointers instead of recursing.

followUpsQA.q: Is merge sort stable on a list?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, taking the left node on ties preserves order.

questions.q: Compare singly, doubly, and circular linked lists. Which would you use to implement browser tab/page switching, and why?

answer.oneLiner: A singly linked list carries only a forward pointer so it is memory-efficient but forces you to scan from the beginning to find a previous node. A doubly linked list adds a backward pointer, making it possible to move in either direction and delete a node in O(1) given a reference to it. A circular list connects the tail back to the head, which is ideal for cycling endlessly through items, while browser back and forward navigation fits a doubly linked list because you need to move naturally in both directions.
keyPoints: A singly linked list uses less memory per node and supports forward traversal only, but finding the previous node costs O(n) because you must scan from the head.
A doubly linked list stores both a next and a previous pointer, enabling O(1) insertion and deletion at any position you already hold a reference to, and traversal in both directions.
A circular linked list has its last node point back to the first, which makes it a natural fit for round-robin scheduling or any scenario where you want to loop through items endlessly.
Browser back and forward navigation is best modeled with a doubly linked list or two stacks, while an endless tab cycling feature maps naturally onto a circular list.

code.note: Doubly linked -> O(1) Back/Forward

dialogue.text: Compare singly, doubly, and circular linked lists. Which fits browser tab/page switching?
Singly has one forward pointer and least memory; doubly adds a prev pointer for O(1) bidirectional moves; circular links tail back to head for natural cycling.
So for browser back/forward navigation, which do you pick?
A doubly linked list, so Back and Forward are both O(1); two stacks also model history cleanly.
And for cycling through open tabs endlessly?
A circular list, since the last tab wraps to the first for round-robin Ctrl+Tab behavior.

followUpsQA.q: When are two stacks simpler than a doubly linked list?
followUpsQA.a: For pure back/forward history with no random deletion.

followUpsQA.q: How does an LRU cache combine a doubly linked list with a hash map?
followUpsQA.a: Map gives O(1) lookup; the list orders recency for O(1) eviction.

followUpsQA.q: What's the cost of the prev pointer?
followUpsQA.a: Extra memory per node in exchange for O(1) reverse traversal.

questions.q: What is the difference between a Tuple and a List in C#? When would you use each?

answer.oneLiner: A Tuple groups a fixed number of values that can each be a different type, accessed by position or name, and is designed for cases like returning multiple values from a method without defining a whole class. A List of T is a dynamically resizable collection where every element shares the same type, built for iteration, searching, and growing over time. The key mental model is that a tuple is a record of related but heterogeneous pieces, while a list is a sequence of uniform items.
keyPoints: A Tuple has a fixed length determined at creation time and can hold values of mixed types, making it well suited for bundling a small number of related return values together.
A List of T has a dynamic length that grows or shrinks as you add or remove items, and every element in the list must be the same type.
The modern ValueTuple syntax using parentheses is a value type that supports named members and lives on the stack, whereas List of T is a reference type allocated on the heap.
Tuples are not designed to be iterated over in a loop the way lists are, and iterating a tuple requires reflection or deconstruction rather than a simple foreach.

code.note: Tuple = fixed heterogeneous; List = dynamic homogeneous

dialogue.text: What's the difference between a Tuple and a List in C#, and when do you use each?
A tuple is a fixed-size, heterogeneous group accessed by position or name; a List<T> is a resizable, homogeneous sequence you index and iterate.
Give me a concrete use case for each.
A tuple for returning multiple values from a method, like `(int count, string name)`; a list for a growable collection of the same type you loop over.
Any value-vs-reference distinction worth noting?
ValueTuple is a value type with named members, while List<T> is a reference type on the heap; tuples aren't meant to be iterated, lists are.

followUpsQA.a: ValueTuple is a mutable value type with named fields; System.Tuple is an immutable reference type.

followUpsQA.q: When prefer a record/class over a tuple?
followUpsQA.a: When the fields need names, methods, or wide reuse for readability.
Yes, var (count, name) = GetStats(); unpacks them.

questions.q: Explain the difference between the stack and the heap in .NET memory management.

answer.oneLiner: The stack is a small, fast, per-thread region of memory that automatically grows and shrinks as methods are called and returned, holding local variables, parameters, and the return address for each frame. The heap is a much larger shared region where objects created with new live, and the garbage collector periodically reclaims memory from objects that are no longer reachable. Value types typically live on the stack or inline inside other objects, while reference type instances always live on the heap.
keyPoints: The stack operates in last-in first-out order and is extremely fast because allocation and deallocation are just pointer adjustments, with memory freed automatically when a method returns.
The heap is shared across threads and holds all class instances and arrays, with the garbage collector responsible for detecting and freeing objects that nothing references anymore.
A variable that holds a reference type sits on the stack and stores the address pointing to the actual object data, which lives on the heap.
Boxing wraps a value type inside an object on the heap so it can be treated as a reference type, and this incurs a heap allocation and a copy, which adds overhead if done frequently.

code.note: Stack: auto-freed locals; Heap: GC-managed objects

dialogue.text: Explain the difference between the stack and the heap in .NET memory management.
The stack is a fast, per-thread LIFO region for call frames, value-type locals, and references, freed automatically on return; the heap holds reference-type objects managed by the GC.
Where does a reference-type variable actually live?
The reference itself sits on the stack, but the object it points to lives on the heap.
What's a common hidden allocation people miss?
Boxing: assigning a value type to object copies it onto the heap, adding allocation and GC pressure.

followUpsQA.q: What causes a StackOverflowException vs an OutOfMemoryException?
followUpsQA.a: Too-deep recursion exhausts the stack; too many live heap objects exhaust memory.

followUpsQA.q: How do generational GC and the LOH work?
followUpsQA.a: Gen 0/1/2 collect short-lived objects cheaply; large objects go to the LOH, collected less often.

followUpsQA.q: Why do stackalloc / Span<T> reduce heap pressure?
followUpsQA.a: They keep buffers on the stack, avoiding heap allocation and GC.

questions.q: Write a query to find the second highest salary from an Employee(Id, Name, Salary) table.

answer.oneLiner: My approach is to rank all distinct salaries using DENSE_RANK in descending order and then filter for rank 2. I use DENSE_RANK rather than RANK because it assigns the same rank to ties without leaving gaps, so if two employees share the top salary they both get rank 1 and the next distinct value correctly gets rank 2. If there is no second distinct salary the query returns NULL, which is the right behavior.
keyPoints: DENSE_RANK assigns the same rank to equal salaries and does not skip numbers, so ties are handled cleanly.
After ranking, filter the result set to rows where the rank equals 2 to isolate the second highest salary.
A subquery approach using MAX where salary is less than the overall MAX also works but is harder to generalize to N-th highest.

code.snippet:
-- Using DENSE_RANK
SELECT MAX(Salary) AS SecondHighestSalary
FROM (
    SELECT Salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS rnk
    FROM Employee
) t
WHERE rnk = 2;

-- Equivalent subquery form (returns NULL if none)
SELECT MAX(Salary) AS SecondHighestSalary
FROM Employee
WHERE Salary < (SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM Employee);
code.note: DENSE_RANK handles duplicate salaries correctly

dialogue.text: Write a query to find the second highest salary.
I'd rank distinct salaries with DENSE_RANK() descending and pick rank 2; a MAX-where-salary-less-than-MAX subquery also works.
Why DENSE_RANK rather than ROW_NUMBER?
DENSE_RANK gives tied salaries the same rank with no gaps, so duplicate top salaries don't push the real second place out.
What happens when there's only one salary?
Rank 2 returns no row, and wrapping in MAX yields NULL rather than erroring, which is the expected behavior.

followUpsQA.a: DENSE_RANK handles ties without gaps; ROW_NUMBER would skip duplicates.

followUpsQA.q: What does it return when only one salary exists?
followUpsQA.a: NULL, because rank 2 has no rows.

followUpsQA.q: How do you generalize to the N-th highest?
followUpsQA.a: Filter rnk = N instead of 2.

questions.q: Generalize: write a query (or function) to find the N-th highest salary.

answer.oneLiner: I apply the same DENSE_RANK pattern as the second-highest solution but parameterize the rank filter with N, which I typically wrap in a stored function so callers can pass any N. The sort that drives DENSE_RANK runs in O(n log n) time. If the table has fewer than N distinct salary values the query returns NULL gracefully.
keyPoints: The pattern is identical to finding the second highest salary except the rank filter uses a parameter N instead of the hard-coded value 2.
Wrapping the query in a function lets you call it with any N without rewriting the SQL each time.
OFFSET/FETCH is a simpler alternative but it does not deduplicate ties, so it can return a different answer than DENSE_RANK when salaries repeat.
Always handle the case where fewer than N distinct salaries exist by ensuring the query returns NULL rather than an error.

code.note: Sort-bound: O(n log n)

dialogue.text: Generalize the second-highest query to the N-th highest salary.
Same DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) pattern, just filter rnk = N, and I'd wrap it in a function so N is a parameter.
Could you use OFFSET/FETCH instead?
Yes, but OFFSET counts raw rows, so duplicate salaries would skew it; I'd need DISTINCT salaries first to match the dedupe semantics.
Complexity and the empty case?
It's O(n log n) from the sort, and when fewer than N distinct salaries exist the MAX wrapper returns NULL gracefully.

followUpsQA.q: How does OFFSET N-1 FETCH 1 differ with duplicate salaries?
followUpsQA.a: It counts rows, not distinct values, so it can land on a tie unless you DISTINCT first.

followUpsQA.q: How do you get per-department N-th highest?
followUpsQA.a: Add PARTITION BY DepartmentId to the window.
The ORDER BY sort, O(n log n).

questions.q: Give an example of a correlated subquery and explain how it differs from a plain subquery.

answer.oneLiner: A correlated subquery is one where the inner query references a column from the outer query, which means it is logically re-executed for every row the outer query visits. The classic example is finding employees who earn more than the average salary of their own department, where the inner query calculates the average for whichever department the current outer row belongs to. It is a clean way to express per-group comparisons, though it can be rewritten as a join or window function for better performance on large tables.
keyPoints: The inner query references a column from the outer query, such as the employee's department ID, which creates the correlation.
Because of that dependency the inner query is logically re-evaluated once per outer row, unlike a plain subquery that runs only once.
A non-correlated subquery has no reference to the outer query and can be evaluated independently before the outer query runs.
Correlated subqueries are often rewritable as a JOIN with a grouped subquery or as a window function, which the optimizer can execute more efficiently.

code.snippet:
-- Correlated: inner query uses outer alias e
SELECT e.Name, e.Salary, e.DepartmentId
FROM Employee e
WHERE e.Salary > (
    SELECT AVG(e2.Salary)
    FROM Employee e2
    WHERE e2.DepartmentId = e.DepartmentId   -- correlation
);

-- Window-function rewrite (usually faster)
SELECT Name, Salary, DepartmentId
FROM (
    SELECT Name, Salary, DepartmentId,
           AVG(Salary) OVER (PARTITION BY DepartmentId) AS deptAvg
    FROM Employee
) t
WHERE Salary > deptAvg;
code.note: Correlated subquery references the outer row

dialogue.text: Give an example of a correlated subquery and how it differs from a plain one.
A correlated subquery references a column from the outer query, like finding employees who earn above their own department's average; the inner query depends on each outer row.
How does that differ from a plain subquery?
A plain subquery runs once, independently, and its result plugs in; a correlated one is logically re-evaluated per outer row.
Any performance concern, and a fix?
Row-by-row evaluation can be slow, so I'd often rewrite it as a window function with AVG() OVER (PARTITION BY DepartmentId).

followUpsQA.q: Why can correlated subqueries be slow?
followUpsQA.a: They re-evaluate per outer row instead of once.

followUpsQA.q: When does EXISTS read better than IN?
followUpsQA.a: EXISTS short-circuits and handles NULLs more predictably than IN.

followUpsQA.q: How do you avoid the correlation entirely?
followUpsQA.a: Rewrite with a window function or a pre-aggregated join.

questions.q: Write a self join: list each employee alongside their manager's name from an Employee(Id, Name, ManagerId) table.

answer.oneLiner: I join the Employee table to itself using two aliases, one representing the employee and one representing the manager, matching each employee's ManagerId to the manager's Id. Using a LEFT JOIN rather than an INNER JOIN ensures that the top-level executive, who has no manager and therefore a NULL ManagerId, still appears in the result set.
keyPoints: Two aliases of the same table are used: one alias for the employee row and another alias for the manager row.
The join condition matches the employee's ManagerId column to the manager alias's Id column.
A LEFT JOIN is used so that employees with a NULL ManagerId, such as the CEO, are still included in the output rather than being filtered out.

code.snippet:
SELECT e.Name AS Employee,
       m.Name AS Manager
FROM Employee e
LEFT JOIN Employee m ON e.ManagerId = m.Id
ORDER BY e.Name;

-- Sample:
-- Alice (ManagerId NULL) -> Manager: NULL (she's the boss)
-- Bob   (ManagerId = Alice.Id) -> Manager: Alice
code.note: LEFT JOIN keeps the manager-less top of the hierarchy

dialogue.text: Write a self join listing each employee alongside their manager's name.
I'd alias the Employee table twice, e for employee and m for manager, joining on e.ManagerId = m.Id.
What about the top boss with no manager?
I use a LEFT JOIN so an employee with a NULL ManagerId still appears, with a NULL manager name.
What if I asked for the whole chain, not just one level up?
Then a single self-join isn't enough; I'd use a recursive CTE to walk the full hierarchy.

followUpsQA.q: INNER vs LEFT join here, who gets dropped?
followUpsQA.a: INNER drops the manager-less top boss; LEFT keeps everyone.

followUpsQA.q: How do you traverse the full hierarchy?
followUpsQA.a: A recursive CTE that joins each level to the next.

followUpsQA.q: How do you count direct reports per manager?
followUpsQA.a: GROUP BY m.Id with COUNT(*).

questions.q: Find duplicate records in a table — rows that share the same values across one or more columns. (Asked at Amazon.)

answer.oneLiner: I group by the combination of columns that together define a unique record and then use HAVING to keep only the groups where the count is greater than one, which means more than one row shares those values. This directly identifies every set of duplicates along with how many times each appears.
keyPoints: GROUP BY the columns that together should uniquely identify a record, which are the candidate key columns for the duplicate check.
HAVING COUNT(*) greater than 1 filters to only those groups where the same combination of values appears more than once.
Including COUNT(*) in the SELECT list tells you exactly how many times each duplicate value set appears in the table.

code.snippet:
SELECT column1, column2, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM your_table
GROUP BY column1, column2
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;
code.note: HAVING filters the aggregated groups

dialogue.text: How would you find duplicate records in a table?
Group by the columns that define a duplicate and keep groups with COUNT(*) > 1.
How would you then delete all but one of each?
Use ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY those columns ORDER BY id) and delete rows where the number is greater than 1.

followUpsQA.a: The count only exists after grouping; WHERE filters pre-aggregation rows.

followUpsQA.q: How to keep one copy when deleting?
followUpsQA.a: ROW_NUMBER per group and delete rn > 1.

questions.q: Find employees who are not assigned to any department, using a LEFT JOIN. Employee(id, name, department_id), Department(department_id, name). (Asked at Uber.)

answer.oneLiner: I LEFT JOIN the Employee table to the Department table so that every employee is kept in the result regardless of whether a matching department exists. Then I filter to rows where the department column is NULL, which identifies employees that had no match on the join and therefore have no assigned department. This pattern is known as an anti-join.
keyPoints: A LEFT JOIN preserves every row from the left table, which is the Employee table, even when there is no matching row in the Department table.
After the join, filtering where the department ID from the right table is NULL isolates the employees who had no department match.

code.snippet:
SELECT e.*
FROM Employee e
LEFT JOIN Department d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
WHERE d.department_id IS NULL;
code.note: LEFT JOIN + IS NULL = anti-join

dialogue.text: Find employees with no department using a LEFT JOIN.
I LEFT JOIN Department and filter WHERE the department side IS NULL, which keeps only employees without a match.
Could you do this with NOT IN or NOT EXISTS?
Yes; NOT EXISTS is usually safest because NOT IN misbehaves if the subquery returns NULLs.

followUpsQA.q: Why filter on the joined key being NULL?
followUpsQA.a: Unmatched left rows get NULLs on the right, marking the absence of a department.
A single NULL in the subquery makes NOT IN return nothing.

questions.q: Calculate the total revenue per product. Sales(product_id, quantity, price). (Asked at PayPal.)

answer.oneLiner: I multiply quantity by unit price to get revenue per row and then SUM those values grouped by product to get the total revenue each product has generated. It is a straightforward aggregation that the database can execute in a single pass through the sales data.
keyPoints: Revenue for each individual row is calculated by multiplying the quantity sold by the price.
Wrapping that expression in SUM and grouping by product ID accumulates the total revenue per product across all its sales rows.

code.note: Multiply per row, then SUM per group

dialogue.text: Total revenue per product?
SUM(quantity * price) grouped by product_id.
What if price lives in a separate Product table?
Join Sales to Product on product_id first, then apply the same SUM and GROUP BY.

followUpsQA.a: Each row's revenue is qty*price; summing the products gives the total.
JOIN to Product and include its name in SELECT and GROUP BY.

questions.q: Get the top 3 highest-paid employees. Employee(id, name, salary). (Asked at Google.)

answer.oneLiner: I order the employee rows by salary in descending order and then limit the result to the first three rows using TOP in SQL Server or LIMIT in MySQL and PostgreSQL. When there are ties at the boundary I can add WITH TIES to include all employees who share the third-place salary rather than arbitrarily cutting some off.
keyPoints: ORDER BY salary descending followed by TOP 3 in SQL Server or LIMIT 3 in MySQL and PostgreSQL retrieves the three highest-paid employees.
When multiple employees share the same salary at the boundary, TOP 3 WITH TIES in SQL Server or a DENSE_RANK filter ensures all tied employees at that salary level are included.

code.snippet:
-- SQL Server
SELECT TOP 3 *
FROM Employee
ORDER BY salary DESC;

-- MySQL / Postgres
SELECT *
FROM Employee
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 3;
code.note: TOP vs LIMIT depending on engine

dialogue.text: Top 3 highest-paid employees.
ORDER BY salary DESC and TOP 3 in SQL Server or LIMIT 3 elsewhere.
What if the 3rd and 4th salaries tie?
Plain TOP 3 cuts arbitrarily; TOP 3 WITH TIES, or DENSE_RANK <= 3, keeps all tied employees.

followUpsQA.a: TOP is T-SQL; LIMIT is MySQL/Postgres — same intent.
DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) <= 3.

questions.q: Find customers who made purchases but never returned any product. Customers(customer_id), Orders(customer_id), Returns(customer_id). (Asked at Walmart.)

answer.oneLiner: I join the Customers table to the Orders table to isolate customers who have actually made a purchase, and then I exclude anyone who also appears in the Returns table. Using NOT EXISTS or NOT IN for that exclusion gives me the set of buyers who have never returned anything.
keyPoints: Joining Customers to Orders first restricts the result to customers who have placed at least one order.
Excluding customers who appear in the Returns table using NOT IN or NOT EXISTS identifies the ones who purchased but never returned anything.
DISTINCT is needed because a single customer can have multiple orders, which would otherwise produce duplicate customer rows in the result.

code.snippet:
SELECT DISTINCT c.customer_id
FROM Customers c
JOIN Orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
WHERE c.customer_id NOT IN (SELECT customer_id FROM Returns);
code.note: Bought (in Orders) minus returned (in Returns)

dialogue.text: Customers who purchased but never returned anything.
Join Customers to Orders so they're buyers, then exclude any customer_id that appears in Returns.
Why prefer NOT EXISTS over NOT IN here?
If Returns.customer_id can be NULL, NOT IN returns nothing; NOT EXISTS handles NULLs correctly.

followUpsQA.a: A customer may have many orders, which would repeat their id.
EXCEPT between the Orders customer set and the Returns customer set.

questions.q: Show the count of orders per customer. Orders(order_id, customer_id). (Asked at Meta.)

answer.oneLiner: I group the orders by customer ID and use COUNT to tally how many orders each customer has placed. If I also need to show customers who have placed zero orders I LEFT JOIN from the Customers table first so those customers are not excluded by the aggregation.
keyPoints: COUNT(*) with GROUP BY customer ID counts the number of order rows belonging to each customer.
If the requirement includes showing customers with zero orders, start with a LEFT JOIN from a Customers table into Orders so unmatched customers still appear in the result with a count of zero.

code.note: Basic grouped count

dialogue.text: Orders per customer.
GROUP BY customer_id with COUNT(*).
How to include customers with zero orders?
LEFT JOIN from Customers to Orders and COUNT(o.order_id), which is 0 for non-matching customers.

followUpsQA.a: Same here, but COUNT(col) ignores NULLs — relevant after a LEFT JOIN.

followUpsQA.q: Show only customers above N orders?
followUpsQA.a: Add HAVING COUNT(*) > N.

questions.q: Retrieve all employees who joined in 2023. Employee(id, name, hire_date). (Asked at Amazon.)

answer.oneLiner: I filter on hire_date using a range predicate that checks whether the date falls on or after January 1 2023 and before January 1 2024. This approach is index-friendly, or sargable, because the column appears alone on one side of the comparison. Wrapping the column in a YEAR() function would prevent the database from using an index on that column.
keyPoints: Using YEAR(hire_date) = 2023 is readable and intuitive but prevents the database from using an index on the hire_date column.
A range predicate checking that hire_date is greater than or equal to 2023-01-01 and strictly less than 2024-01-01 is sargable and allows index usage.

code.snippet:
-- Simple
SELECT *
FROM Employee
WHERE YEAR(hire_date) = 2023;

-- Sargable (index-friendly)
SELECT *
FROM Employee
WHERE hire_date >= '2023-01-01'
  AND hire_date <  '2024-01-01';
code.note: Range form keeps an index on hire_date usable

dialogue.text: Employees hired in 2023.
WHERE YEAR(hire_date) = 2023, though I'd prefer a date range so an index on hire_date is usable.
Why does YEAR(hire_date) hurt index usage?
Wrapping the column in a function makes the predicate non-sargable, forcing a scan; the range form compares the raw column.

followUpsQA.a: One that can use an index — the column appears bare, not inside a function.
The half-open bound correctly includes any time component on Dec 31.

questions.q: Calculate the average order value per customer. Orders(customer_id, total_amount). (Asked at Microsoft.)

answer.oneLiner: I group the orders by customer ID and apply AVG to the order amount, which gives the mean order value for each customer. It is worth noting that AVG ignores NULL values, so if any order amounts are NULL and should be treated as zero I would wrap them in COALESCE before averaging.
keyPoints: AVG of the total amount column grouped by customer ID calculates the mean order value per customer.
AVG automatically ignores NULL values, so use COALESCE to convert NULLs to zero first if a NULL amount should be counted as a zero-value order.

code.note: AVG per group

dialogue.text: Average order value per customer.
GROUP BY customer_id with AVG(total_amount).
How does AVG treat NULL amounts?
AVG skips NULLs entirely; if a NULL should count as zero I'd AVG(COALESCE(total_amount,0)).

followUpsQA.a: AVG = SUM/COUNT of non-NULLs; they match unless NULLs are present.
Wrap in ROUND(AVG(...), 2).

questions.q: Get the latest order placed by each customer. Orders(customer_id, order_date). (Asked at Uber.)

answer.oneLiner: If I only need the most recent date I group by customer and take MAX of order_date, which is simple and efficient. If I need the full row for the latest order I use ROW_NUMBER partitioned by customer and ordered by order_date descending, then filter to row number 1, which gives me exactly one complete row per customer.
keyPoints: MAX of order_date grouped by customer ID is the simplest way to find the most recent order date for each customer.
To return the complete latest order row, use ROW_NUMBER partitioned by customer ID and ordered by order_date descending, then filter to rows where the row number equals 1.

code.snippet:
-- Latest date only
SELECT customer_id, MAX(order_date) AS latest_order_date
FROM Orders
GROUP BY customer_id;

-- Full latest row
SELECT customer_id, order_id, order_date
FROM (
    SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC) AS rn
    FROM Orders
) t
WHERE rn = 1;
code.note: MAX for the date; ROW_NUMBER for the whole row

dialogue.text: Latest order per customer.
If they just want the date, MAX(order_date) grouped by customer; if they want the order's other columns, ROW_NUMBER per customer ordered by date desc and keep rn = 1.
Why can't plain GROUP BY return the order_id too?
MAX(order_date) doesn't tie back to a specific row's order_id, so I need the window approach to keep the matching row.

followUpsQA.q: Two orders on the same latest date?
followUpsQA.a: ROW_NUMBER picks one; add a tiebreaker like order_id DESC to make it deterministic.
ROW_NUMBER guarantees a single row; RANK could return ties.

questions.q: Find products that were never sold. Products(product_id), Sales(product_id). (Asked at Adobe.)

answer.oneLiner: I LEFT JOIN the Products table to the Sales table so that every product is present in the result even if it has never appeared in a sale. Then I filter to rows where the Sales side of the join is NULL, which means no matching sale was found for that product.
keyPoints: A LEFT JOIN starting from the Products table preserves every product row regardless of whether a matching sale exists.
Filtering where the product ID from the Sales table is NULL after the join isolates products that have never been sold.

code.snippet:
SELECT p.product_id
FROM Products p
LEFT JOIN Sales s ON p.product_id = s.product_id
WHERE s.product_id IS NULL;
code.note: Anti-join for never-matched products

dialogue.text: Which products were never sold?
LEFT JOIN Products to Sales and keep rows where the Sales side is NULL.
NOT EXISTS instead?
Yes — WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM Sales s WHERE s.product_id = p.product_id) expresses the same and avoids NOT IN's NULL trap.

followUpsQA.q: Why does IS NULL find unsold products?
followUpsQA.a: Unmatched left rows get NULL on the right after a LEFT JOIN.

followUpsQA.q: Performance of LEFT JOIN vs NOT EXISTS?
followUpsQA.a: Often similar; NOT EXISTS can short-circuit and is NULL-safe.

questions.q: Identify the most-selling product by total quantity. Sales(product_id, quantity). (Asked at Walmart.)

answer.oneLiner: I sum the quantity sold grouped by product, order the results by total quantity descending, and take the top row to find the product that sold the most units. If I want to surface all products tied for the top position I use WITH TIES in SQL Server or apply a DENSE_RANK filter.
keyPoints: SUM of quantity grouped by product ID gives the total units sold per product.
Ordering by the total descending and taking the first row with TOP 1 or LIMIT 1 identifies the highest-selling product.
TOP 1 WITH TIES in SQL Server or a DENSE_RANK filter ensures that all products sharing the maximum quantity are included rather than just one arbitrary winner.

code.snippet:
SELECT TOP 1 product_id, SUM(quantity) AS total_qty
FROM Sales
GROUP BY product_id
ORDER BY total_qty DESC;
code.note: Use TOP 1 WITH TIES if leaders can tie

dialogue.text: Most-selling product.
SUM(quantity) per product, ORDER BY that descending, TOP 1.
What if two products tie for first?
TOP 1 picks one arbitrarily; TOP 1 WITH TIES (or a rank = 1 filter) returns both.

followUpsQA.a: SUM(quantity * price) as the ordering key.
ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY total DESC) = 1.

questions.q: Get the total revenue and number of orders per region. Orders(region, total_amount). (Asked at Meta.)

answer.oneLiner: I group by region and compute both SUM of the order amount and COUNT of orders in a single aggregation pass. This gives total revenue and order volume together without requiring two separate queries.
keyPoints: Both metrics, total revenue and order count, are produced by a single GROUP BY region clause, so the table is scanned only once.
SUM of the amount column provides total revenue per region, and COUNT(*) provides the number of orders in each region.

code.snippet:
SELECT region,
       SUM(total_amount) AS total_revenue,
       COUNT(*)          AS order_count
FROM Orders
GROUP BY region;
code.note: Multiple aggregates, one grouping

dialogue.text: Revenue and order count per region.
GROUP BY region with SUM(total_amount) and COUNT(*) together.
Add average order value too?
Include AVG(total_amount), or compute SUM/COUNT, in the same grouped query.

followUpsQA.q: Can you compute several aggregates at once?
followUpsQA.a: Yes — any number of aggregates share one GROUP BY scan.

followUpsQA.q: Only regions above a revenue threshold?
followUpsQA.a: Add HAVING SUM(total_amount) > X.

questions.q: Count how many customers placed more than 5 orders. Orders(customer_id). (Asked at Amazon.)

answer.oneLiner: I first group orders by customer and use HAVING to keep only those with more than five orders, which produces a derived set of high-frequency customers. I then wrap that in an outer COUNT to tally how many customers met that threshold.
keyPoints: The inner query groups by customer ID and applies HAVING COUNT(*) greater than 5 to identify customers who placed more than five orders.
The outer query counts the rows returned by that inner result, giving the total number of customers who exceeded the threshold.

code.snippet:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS customer_count
FROM (
    SELECT customer_id
    FROM Orders
    GROUP BY customer_id
    HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
) AS sub;
code.note: Count the groups that pass HAVING

dialogue.text: How many customers placed more than 5 orders?
I get the qualifying customers with GROUP BY + HAVING COUNT(*) > 5, then COUNT them in an outer query.
Why the nested query?
HAVING filters groups, but I need a count of those groups, which requires a second aggregation over the subquery.

followUpsQA.a: Not cleanly — you're counting groups, which is an aggregate over an aggregate.
Drop the outer query and select customer_id.

questions.q: Retrieve customers (orders) with a value above the overall average order value. Orders(order_id, total_amount). (Asked at PayPal.)

answer.oneLiner: I compare each order's amount against a scalar subquery that calculates the global average order value. Because the subquery returns a single value the database evaluates it once and then applies the comparison to every row, making this an efficient approach for filtering above-average orders.
keyPoints: The subquery selecting AVG of total amount from the Orders table runs once and returns a single scalar value representing the global average.
The WHERE clause compares each order's total amount to that scalar, keeping only orders whose amount exceeds the average.

code.note: Non-correlated scalar subquery, evaluated once

dialogue.text: Orders above the average order value.
WHERE total_amount > (SELECT AVG(total_amount) FROM Orders) — a non-correlated subquery evaluated once.
Is the average recomputed per row?
No — it's non-correlated, so the optimizer computes it once and reuses it.

followUpsQA.a: This is non-correlated; per-customer averages would correlate on customer_id.
Correlate the subquery on customer_id (or use AVG() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id)).

questions.q: Find all employees hired on weekends. Employee(id, hire_date). (Asked at Google.)

answer.oneLiner: To find employees hired on weekends, I use DATENAME to extract the day name from the hire_date and check whether it equals Saturday or Sunday. I prefer DATENAME over DATEPART with a numeric weekday value because DATENAME returns a string like Saturday or Sunday that is unambiguous across database servers, whereas DATEPART returns a locale-sensitive number where what counts as day 1 can vary depending on the DATEFIRST setting.
keyPoints: DATENAME of WEEKDAY returns the full day name as a string, like Monday or Saturday, which makes the filter self-documenting and avoids locale confusion.

Using DATEPART with a numeric value like 1 or 7 to mean Sunday or Saturday is fragile because the SET DATEFIRST setting controls what number each day gets, so the same query can return different results on different servers.
This predicate wraps the column in a function, which means it is not sargable — the database cannot use a standard index on hire_date and will do a full scan instead.

On MySQL you would use DAYOFWEEK returning 1 for Sunday and 7 for Saturday, or WEEKDAY returning 5 for Saturday and 6 for Sunday; on PostgreSQL you would use EXTRACT with DOW where 0 is Sunday and 6 is Saturday.

code.note: DATENAME is clearer than DATEPART's locale-sensitive number

dialogue.text: Employees hired on a weekend.
Filter where DATENAME(WEEKDAY, hire_date) is Saturday or Sunday.
Why not DATEPART(WEEKDAY, ...) = 1 or 7?
DATEPART's numbering depends on DATEFIRST/locale, so DATENAME by name is safer; in MySQL I'd use DAYOFWEEK or WEEKDAY.

followUpsQA.a: MySQL WEEKDAY() (5,6) or DAYOFWEEK() (1,7); Postgres EXTRACT(DOW...) (0,6).
No — the function on the column blocks index use; a computed/persisted column would help.

questions.q: Find all employees with a salary between 50000 and 100000. Employee(id, salary). (Asked at Microsoft.)

answer.oneLiner: The simplest way to filter a numeric range in SQL is the BETWEEN operator, and the important thing to remember is that it is inclusive on both ends, meaning a salary of exactly 50000 or exactly 100000 would be included in the result. If the interviewer wanted exclusive bounds, I would switch to explicit greater-than and less-than comparisons instead.
keyPoints: BETWEEN 50000 AND 100000 includes both endpoints, so an employee earning exactly 50000 or exactly 100000 qualifies.
The expression is equivalent to writing salary greater than or equal to 50000 AND salary less than or equal to 100000.
For exclusive bounds you would write salary greater than 50000 AND salary less than 100000, dropping the equals signs.
When using BETWEEN with date values, be careful at the upper boundary: if the column stores a datetime with a time component, rows from late on the last day may be missed if you only specify a date.

code.note: BETWEEN is inclusive on both ends

dialogue.text: Employees earning 50k to 100k.
WHERE salary BETWEEN 50000 AND 100000, which is inclusive of both bounds.
If they wanted exclusive bounds?
I'd switch to salary > 50000 AND salary < 100000 instead of BETWEEN.

followUpsQA.a: Yes, both endpoints are included.
Be careful — 'BETWEEN date1 AND date2' includes midnight of date2 only, missing later times that day.

questions.q: Get monthly sales revenue and order count. Orders(order_id, date, amount). (Asked at Google.)

answer.oneLiner: The key here is grouping by year and month together so that the same month in different years does not get merged. I use FORMAT with a yyyy-MM pattern in SQL Server to produce a sortable year-month string, then compute SUM of the amount and COUNT of the order IDs within each group. Ordering by the formatted month string works correctly because the yyyy-MM format sorts lexicographically in the right chronological order.
keyPoints: If you group by MONTH alone you collapse all Januaries across all years into a single bucket, which is almost never what the interviewer wants when asking for monthly trends.
FORMAT with the pattern yyyy-MM produces a string like 2023-01 that sorts correctly as text, so an ORDER BY on that column gives chronological order without needing extra date arithmetic.
SUM of the amount gives total revenue and COUNT of the order ID gives the number of orders, and both aggregate over the same grouping in a single pass through the data.
On MySQL you would use DATE_FORMAT with percent-Y dash percent-m, and on PostgreSQL you would use TO_CHAR with YYYY-MM.

code.snippet:
SELECT FORMAT(date, 'yyyy-MM') AS month,
       SUM(amount)        AS total_revenue,
       COUNT(order_id)    AS order_count
FROM Orders
GROUP BY FORMAT(date, 'yyyy-MM')
ORDER BY month;
code.note: Group by year-month string

dialogue.text: Monthly revenue and order counts.
Group by FORMAT(date,'yyyy-MM') and take SUM(amount) and COUNT(order_id).
Why include the year in the bucket?
Grouping by month alone would merge the same month across different years.

followUpsQA.a: MySQL DATE_FORMAT(date,'%Y-%m'); Postgres TO_CHAR(date,'YYYY-MM').
The 'yyyy-MM' string sorts correctly lexicographically.

questions.q: Rank employees by salary within each department. Employee(employee_id, department_id, salary). (Asked at Amazon.)

answer.oneLiner: To rank employees by salary within each department independently, I use a window function called RANK with a PARTITION BY clause on the department ID. The PARTITION BY resets the ranking counter every time the department changes, so the top earner in each department gets rank 1 regardless of how employees in other departments are paid. The ORDER BY inside the window defines the direction of ranking, so ORDER BY salary descending means the highest salary gets rank 1.
keyPoints: The PARTITION BY clause is what makes the ranking restart for each department; without it you would get a single global ranking across all employees.
RANK assigns the same rank number to employees who share a salary, and then skips the next number, so if two people tie for rank 2 the next person gets rank 4.
DENSE_RANK is similar to RANK but does not skip numbers after a tie, so a tie at rank 2 means the next distinct salary is rank 3 rather than rank 4.
ROW_NUMBER assigns a unique sequential number to every row with no ties allowed, which is useful when you need exactly one row per rank position.

code.note: PARTITION BY = per-department ranking

dialogue.text: Rank employees by salary within each department.
RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY salary DESC); the partition restarts ranks per department.
RANK vs DENSE_RANK vs ROW_NUMBER?
RANK skips numbers after ties, DENSE_RANK keeps them contiguous, and ROW_NUMBER assigns a unique sequence regardless of ties.

followUpsQA.a: When you need exactly one row per position, e.g. picking a single top earner.

followUpsQA.q: Top earner per department from this?
followUpsQA.a: Wrap it and filter salary_rk = 1.

questions.q: Find customers who placed orders in every month of 2023. Orders(customer_id, order_date). (Asked at Meta.)

answer.oneLiner: The approach is to filter orders to the year 2023, group by customer, and then use HAVING with COUNT of distinct months to keep only customers who have an order in all twelve months. The key insight is that you need COUNT of DISTINCT months rather than COUNT of orders, because a customer who places multiple orders in January but nothing in October would have a high order count but would not qualify.
keyPoints: Filtering with YEAR of order_date equal to 2023 first narrows the data to the relevant year before grouping.
COUNT of DISTINCT months within each customer's orders tells you how many unique months they were active, which is different from how many total orders they placed.
HAVING COUNT of distinct months equal to 12 keeps only customers who placed at least one order in every calendar month of the year.

Formatting the order date to a year-month string before counting DISTINCT ensures that months from different years would not collapse together, which is a useful habit even when you have already filtered to one year.

code.snippet:
SELECT customer_id
FROM Orders
WHERE YEAR(order_date) = 2023
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT FORMAT(order_date, 'yyyy-MM')) = 12;
code.note: 12 distinct months = ordered every month

dialogue.text: Customers who ordered in all 12 months of 2023.
Filter to 2023, group by customer, and keep those with COUNT(DISTINCT month) = 12.
Why DISTINCT on the month?
A customer could place many orders in one month; DISTINCT counts months covered, not orders.

followUpsQA.a: Within a single year MONTH() also works; FORMAT generalizes across years.
Change the HAVING comparison to >= N.

questions.q: Find the moving average of sales over the last 3 days. Orders(order_date, total_amount). (Asked at Microsoft.)

answer.oneLiner: A moving average is computed using a window function with an explicit frame that includes a fixed number of preceding rows. For a 3-day moving average I use AVG of total amount with an OVER clause ordered by order date and a ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW frame, which averages the current day and the two days immediately before it. The ROWS keyword counts physical rows in the result, which is what you want here.
keyPoints: The frame clause ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW tells the database to include the current row and exactly two rows before it in the calculation, giving a window of three rows.

Using ROWS rather than RANGE is important when multiple rows share the same order date, because RANGE groups all rows with the same order value together and would include or exclude entire days at once rather than exactly three rows.

The first two rows in the result will have smaller averages based on fewer data points since there are not yet two preceding rows available, so if the interviewer wants a full 7-day window they should filter out early rows.
If multiple orders exist on the same date and you want a true calendar-day moving average, aggregate to one row per day first before applying the window function.

code.note: 3-row trailing window via ROWS frame

dialogue.text: 3-day moving average of sales.
AVG(total_amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) — current plus two prior.
Difference between ROWS and RANGE here?
ROWS counts physical rows; RANGE groups peers with equal order_date, so with multiple rows per day they behave differently.

followUpsQA.q: Aggregate to one row per day first?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, if 'last 3 days' means calendar days rather than 3 rows.

followUpsQA.q: Why is the default frame a trap?
followUpsQA.a: With ORDER BY and no explicit frame, it's RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING — a running total, not a moving window.

questions.q: Identify the first and last order date for each customer. Orders(customer_id, order_date). (Asked at Uber.)

answer.oneLiner: This is a straightforward aggregation where you group orders by customer ID and take MIN of order date for the first order and MAX of order date for the last order. Both values come from a single pass through the data in one grouped query, which is efficient. If you also need the order amount or other columns from those specific rows, you would need a different approach using window functions because MIN and MAX only return the date value, not the full row.
keyPoints: MIN of order_date within each customer group returns the earliest date that customer placed an order.
MAX of order_date within the same group returns the most recent order date for that customer.
Because both MIN and MAX share the same GROUP BY, the database scans the data once and computes both aggregates together.

If you need the order amount or order ID from the first or last order row specifically, you cannot use MIN and MAX for that because they only return the date; you would need ROW_NUMBER or FIRST_VALUE window functions to fetch the full row instead.

code.snippet:
SELECT customer_id,
       MIN(order_date) AS first_order,
       MAX(order_date) AS last_order
FROM Orders
GROUP BY customer_id;
code.note: MIN/MAX in one pass

dialogue.text: First and last order date per customer.
GROUP BY customer_id with MIN(order_date) and MAX(order_date).
Also want the amounts of those first/last orders?
Then I'd switch to window functions (FIRST_VALUE/LAST_VALUE or ROW_NUMBER) to carry the matching row's other columns.

followUpsQA.a: DATEDIFF(DAY, MIN(order_date), MAX(order_date)).

followUpsQA.q: Why can't MIN/MAX return the amount too?
followUpsQA.a: They aggregate independently and aren't tied to one row.

questions.q: Show product sales distribution as a percentage of total revenue. Sales(product_id, quantity, price). (Asked at PayPal.)

answer.oneLiner: To compute each product's share of total revenue, I calculate the overall total revenue once in a CTE and then cross join it into the per-product aggregation so I can divide each product's revenue by the grand total. I multiply by 100.0 rather than 100 to force the database to treat the division as floating point arithmetic, because dividing two integer columns would give an integer result and truncate the decimal portion.
keyPoints: A CTE that computes SUM of quantity times price across all products gives you the grand total revenue as a single scalar value.
CROSS JOIN attaches that single grand total to every row in the per-product aggregation, which is exactly what you want since you are dividing each product's revenue by the same denominator.
Multiplying by 100.0 rather than 100 promotes the expression to floating point arithmetic so the percentage retains its decimal places instead of being truncated to a whole number.
An alternative to the CROSS JOIN approach is to use a window function with SUM over an empty OVER clause, which computes the total across all rows without needing a separate CTE.

code.snippet:
WITH TotalRevenue AS (
    SELECT SUM(quantity * price) AS total FROM Sales
)
SELECT s.product_id,
       SUM(s.quantity * s.price) AS revenue,
       SUM(s.quantity * s.price) * 100.0 / t.total AS revenue_pct
FROM Sales s
CROSS JOIN TotalRevenue t
GROUP BY s.product_id, t.total;
code.note: 100.0 avoids integer-division truncation

dialogue.text: Each product's share of total revenue.
I compute the grand total once in a CTE, cross join it in, and divide each product's revenue by it times 100.0.
Why 100.0 rather than 100?
With integer columns, * 100 / total would do integer division and truncate; the decimal literal promotes the expression to floating point.

followUpsQA.q: Window alternative to the CROSS JOIN?
followUpsQA.a: revenue * 100.0 / SUM(revenue) OVER () after aggregating per product.
It's a constant, but SQL requires every non-aggregated SELECT column in GROUP BY.

questions.q: Retrieve customers who made purchases on two consecutive days. Orders(id, order_date). (Asked at Walmart.)

answer.oneLiner: To find customers who purchased on two consecutive days, I use the LAG window function to bring each customer's previous order date into the same row as the current order, and then I filter to rows where the day difference between those two dates is exactly one. I partition the window by customer so that each customer's orders are compared only against their own history rather than across different customers.
keyPoints: LAG of order_date partitioned by customer ID and ordered by date returns the date of the previous order for that same customer.
DATEDIFF between the previous date and the current date equal to 1 identifies pairs of consecutive-day orders.
Adding DISTINCT at the outer level collapses customers who have multiple qualifying pairs of consecutive-day purchases so each qualifying customer appears only once.

If multiple orders can exist on the same calendar day, you should deduplicate orders to one row per customer per day before applying the LAG, otherwise the difference might be zero and consecutive days could be missed.

code.snippet:
WITH cte AS (
    SELECT customer_id, order_date,
           LAG(order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_date
    FROM Orders
)
SELECT DISTINCT customer_id
FROM cte
WHERE DATEDIFF(DAY, prev_date, order_date) = 1;
code.note: LAG + 1-day diff = consecutive purchases

dialogue.text: Customers who bought on two consecutive days.
LAG the previous order date per customer, then keep rows where the day difference is exactly 1.
Why LAG over a self-join?
LAG reads the prior row directly in one ordered pass, avoiding a self-join and its O(n^2) risk.

followUpsQA.q: Duplicate orders on the same day?
followUpsQA.a: Dedupe to one row per (customer, day) first, or the diff could be 0.
A gaps-and-islands grouping on date minus a row sequence.

questions.q: Find churned customers — those with no orders in the last 6 months. Orders(customer_id, order_date). (Asked at Amazon.)

answer.oneLiner: To identify churned customers I group orders by customer and compute the most recent order date using MAX, then compare that date to a cutoff that is six months before the current date. Customers whose last order falls before that cutoff have not ordered in at least six months and are considered churned. I use HAVING rather than WHERE because I am filtering on an aggregate value, specifically the MAX of the order date, which does not exist until after the grouping step.
keyPoints: MAX of order_date per customer gives you the date of that customer's most recent order, which is what you want to compare against the churn cutoff.
DATEADD with a negative six months applied to GETDATE generates the cutoff date dynamically, so the query stays current without needing a hard-coded date.
HAVING is required here because WHERE filters individual rows before aggregation, but the condition you want to apply is on MAX of order_date, which is a result of aggregation.

Customers who never ordered at all will not appear in the Orders table, so this query only identifies formerly active customers who have gone quiet; you would need a LEFT JOIN from a Customers table to also catch people who signed up but never ordered.

code.snippet:
SELECT customer_id
FROM Orders
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING MAX(order_date) < DATEADD(MONTH, -6, GETDATE());
code.note: Last order older than the 6-month cutoff

dialogue.text: Define and find churned customers (no order in 6 months).
Group by customer and keep those whose MAX(order_date) is before DATEADD(MONTH,-6,GETDATE()).
Why use HAVING with MAX rather than a WHERE filter?
A WHERE on order_date would test individual orders; I need the customer's latest order overall, which is an aggregate.

followUpsQA.a: MySQL DATE_SUB(NOW(), INTERVAL 6 MONTH); Postgres NOW() - INTERVAL '6 months'.
They aren't in Orders; LEFT JOIN from Customers to include them as churned.

questions.q: Calculate cumulative (running) revenue by day. Orders(order_date, total_amount). (Asked at Adobe.)

answer.oneLiner: A cumulative or running total is computed with a SUM window function that uses the default frame, which is everything from the beginning of the partition up to and including the current row. When you write SUM of total_amount with an OVER clause ordered by order date and no explicit frame, the database applies RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW, which accumulates the total from the first row up to each successive row. This gives you a running revenue figure that grows with each row as you move forward in time.
keyPoints: A window SUM with ORDER BY and no ROWS or RANGE clause uses the default frame of RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING TO CURRENT ROW, which is exactly the cumulative total behavior you want.
The word running total and cumulative total mean the same thing here: each row shows the sum of all amounts from the first row up to and including that row.
If multiple orders share the same date, the RANGE default groups all same-date rows together and assigns them all the same cumulative total, which may or may not be what the interviewer intends.
To produce one clean cumulative figure per calendar day, aggregate to daily totals first in a CTE and then apply the running SUM to the daily-total data.

code.note: Default frame with ORDER BY = running total

dialogue.text: Cumulative revenue by day.
SUM(total_amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date); the implicit frame makes it a running total.
What if multiple orders share a date?
With RANGE (the default) same-date rows share a cumulative value; I'd pre-aggregate to one row per day for a clean daily curve.

followUpsQA.q: What's the default frame with ORDER BY?
followUpsQA.a: RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW.
Add PARTITION BY customer_id to the window.

questions.q: Identify top-performing departments by average salary. Employee(department_id, salary). (Asked at Google.)

answer.oneLiner: The query is a simple aggregation: group employees by department and compute the average salary within each group, then order the results by that average descending so the highest-paying departments come first. If you only want the top few departments you add a TOP clause in SQL Server or LIMIT in MySQL or PostgreSQL to truncate the output.
keyPoints: GROUP BY department_id with AVG of salary computes the mean salary for every employee in each department.
Ordering by the average salary descending surfaces the departments with the highest average pay at the top of the result.
If you want to also display the department name, join to a Department table on department ID and include the name column in both the SELECT list and the GROUP BY clause.
To restrict to departments whose average exceeds the company-wide average, add a HAVING clause that compares the department average against a scalar subquery computing the global AVG of salary.

code.snippet:
SELECT department_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM Employee
GROUP BY department_id
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;
code.note: Add TOP N for just the top departments

dialogue.text: Departments ranked by average salary.
GROUP BY department_id with AVG(salary), ordered descending; TOP N if they only want the best few.
Include the department name?
Join a Department table on department_id and add its name to SELECT and GROUP BY.

followUpsQA.a: HAVING AVG(salary) > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM Employee).
Use TOP N WITH TIES or a rank-based filter.

questions.q: Find customers who ordered more than the average number of orders per customer. Orders(customer_id). (Asked at Meta.)

answer.oneLiner: The challenge here is that we want to compare each customer's order count to the average order count across all customers, but the average must be computed over per-customer totals rather than over individual order rows. I solve this with a CTE that first counts orders per customer, then the outer query selects only those customers whose count exceeds the average of counts from the CTE.
keyPoints: The CTE computes a COUNT of orders for each customer using GROUP BY customer ID, giving you one row per customer with their total order count.
The outer query filters the CTE results by comparing each customer's order count to a subquery that computes AVG of the order counts across all customers in the CTE.
