It is important to take the average over the per-customer counts from the CTE rather than over the raw Orders table rows, because the latter would give you the average number of rows per customer ID, which in practice is the same thing but is easier to reason about correctly when framed as an average over totals.
Some database engines truncate the average of integers to a whole number, so if you need precision you should cast the count to a decimal before averaging.

code.snippet:
WITH customer_orders AS (
    SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count
    FROM Orders
    GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT *
FROM customer_orders
WHERE order_count > (SELECT AVG(order_count) FROM customer_orders);
code.note: Average is over per-customer counts, not raw rows

dialogue.text: Customers ordering more than the average customer.
First a CTE of per-customer counts, then keep those above the average of those counts.
Why not AVG over the Orders table directly?
That would average rows, not customers; the average must be taken over the per-customer aggregates.

followUpsQA.a: AVG of integers can truncate in some engines; cast to decimal to be safe.

followUpsQA.q: Reuse the CTE twice — is that a problem?
followUpsQA.a: No; the optimizer can materialize or inline it as needed.

questions.q: Calculate revenue generated from new customers (their first-time orders). Orders(customer_id, order_date, total_amount). (Asked at Microsoft.)

answer.oneLiner: To calculate revenue from first-time orders I find each customer's earliest order date in a CTE using MIN of order date grouped by customer, and then join that back to the Orders table on both the customer ID and the order date to retrieve the actual order rows from that first day. Summing the amounts on those joined rows gives the total revenue generated from new customers' first purchases.
keyPoints: The CTE uses MIN of order_date grouped by customer_id to determine when each customer placed their very first order.

Joining the CTE back to the Orders table on both customer_id and the first order date retrieves the actual order rows from each customer's first day, which could be more than one row if a customer placed multiple orders on their first day.
Summing total_amount on the joined result gives the total revenue attributable to first-time orders.

If you want only the single earliest order per customer rather than all orders placed on the first day, you would use ROW_NUMBER partitioned by customer ordered by order date and keep only the row numbered 1.

code.snippet:
WITH first_orders AS (
    SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
    FROM Orders
    GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT SUM(o.total_amount) AS new_revenue
FROM Orders o
JOIN first_orders f
  ON o.customer_id = f.customer_id
 AND o.order_date  = f.first_order_date;
code.note: Sum only each customer's first order

dialogue.text: Revenue from customers' first-ever orders.
A CTE gets each customer's MIN(order_date); I join Orders back on that date and SUM the amounts.
What if a customer placed two orders on their first day?
Both would match the first date and sum in; if only the very first order counts, I'd rank by a timestamp and keep rank 1.

followUpsQA.a: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer ORDER BY order_date) = 1, then SUM.

followUpsQA.q: New customers in a given month?
followUpsQA.a: Filter first_order_date to that month before summing.

questions.q: Find the percentage of employees in each department. Employee(department_id). (Asked at Uber.)

answer.oneLiner: To compute the percentage of employees in each department, I count employees grouped by department and divide each count by the total employee count from a scalar subquery. Multiplying by 100.0 rather than 100 ensures the division produces a decimal result rather than an integer because integer division would truncate the fraction to zero.
keyPoints: COUNT of all rows grouped by department_id gives the number of employees in each department.
The denominator is a scalar subquery that computes the total count of all employees in the company, and because it is non-correlated it is evaluated once and reused for every department row.
Multiplying by 100.0 before dividing promotes the expression to floating point arithmetic, which preserves the fractional part of the percentage.
An alternative approach uses a window function: COUNT of all rows in the SELECT, divided by SUM of COUNT over an empty OVER clause, which computes the total without a separate subquery.

code.snippet:
SELECT department_id,
       COUNT(*) AS emp_count,
       COUNT(*) * 100.0 / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Employee) AS pct
FROM Employee
GROUP BY department_id;
code.note: 100.0 forces decimal division

dialogue.text: Percentage of employees per department.
COUNT(*) per department times 100.0 divided by the total employee count from a scalar subquery.
Window-function version?
COUNT(*) * 100.0 / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER () after grouping, which avoids the separate subquery.

followUpsQA.a: Integer division would truncate the ratio to 0; the decimal literal promotes it.
Yes, up to rounding.

questions.q: Retrieve the maximum salary difference within each department. Employee(department_id, salary). (Asked at PayPal.)

answer.oneLiner: The salary spread within a department is simply the difference between the highest and lowest salary in that department, computed by subtracting MIN of salary from MAX of salary in a GROUP BY query. This is one of those cases where the answer is almost deceptively simple, but it is worth mentioning that a department with only one employee would have a spread of zero since MIN and MAX would be the same value.
keyPoints: MAX of salary minus MIN of salary grouped by department gives the range or spread of salaries within each department.
A department with a single employee has MIN and MAX equal to each other, so the difference is zero, which is the correct answer in that case.
Ordering the result by salary difference descending lets you quickly identify which departments have the widest pay gap.

If the interviewer asks which specific employees earn the max and min, you would need to use window functions to compare each row's salary to the MAX and MIN over their partition rather than using a simple aggregation.

code.snippet:
SELECT department_id,
       MAX(salary) - MIN(salary) AS salary_diff
FROM Employee
GROUP BY department_id;
code.note: Range = MAX minus MIN

dialogue.text: Largest salary gap within each department.
MAX(salary) - MIN(salary) grouped by department_id.
What if a department has one employee?
MAX and MIN are equal, so the difference is 0, which is correct.

followUpsQA.q: Identify who earns the max and min?
followUpsQA.a: Use window MAX/MIN OVER (PARTITION BY dept) and compare each row.

followUpsQA.q: Departments with the widest spread first?
followUpsQA.a: ORDER BY salary_diff DESC.

questions.q: Find products that contribute to the top 80% of revenue (Pareto / 80-20). Sales(product_id, qty, price). (Asked at Walmart.)

answer.oneLiner: The Pareto or 80-20 analysis works by ranking products from highest revenue to lowest, computing a running total of revenue, and identifying the products whose cumulative contribution covers the first 80 percent of total revenue. I do this in two CTEs: the first computes per-product revenue, the second computes a running SUM ordered by revenue descending alongside the grand total using a window function. Then an outer query filters to rows where the running total is at or below 80 percent of the grand total.
keyPoints: The first CTE aggregates quantity times price per product to get each product's total revenue.

The second CTE computes two window functions on those product revenues: a running SUM ordered by revenue descending to accumulate from the highest contributor downward, and a global SUM over an empty window to get the grand total.
The outer query filters where the running total is less than or equal to 80 percent of the grand total, which captures all products whose combined revenue makes up the top 80 percent.

Window functions like SUM OVER cannot appear directly in a WHERE clause because the WHERE is evaluated before the window calculations, which is why the filter must go in an outer query that wraps the windowed result.

code.snippet:
WITH sales_cte AS (
    SELECT product_id, SUM(qty * price) AS revenue
    FROM Sales
    GROUP BY product_id
),
ranked AS (
    SELECT product_id, revenue,
           SUM(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY revenue DESC
                ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total,
           SUM(revenue) OVER () AS total
    FROM sales_cte
)
SELECT product_id, revenue
FROM ranked
WHERE running_total <= 0.8 * total;
code.note: Window functions can't sit in WHERE — filter in an outer query

dialogue.text: Which products make up the top 80% of revenue?
I rank products by revenue, keep a running total and the grand total via window sums, then in an outer query keep rows where running_total <= 0.8 * total.
Why can't the running-total comparison go in WHERE?
Window functions are computed after WHERE, so referencing one there is illegal; I push the filter to an outer SELECT.

followUpsQA.a: Compare the previous running total (LAG) to 0.8*total to include the boundary product.

followUpsQA.q: Why ORDER BY revenue DESC in the running sum?
followUpsQA.a: Pareto accumulates from the biggest contributors first.

questions.q: Calculate the average time between two consecutive purchases for each customer. Orders(customer_id, order_date). (Asked at Meta.)

answer.oneLiner: To find the average number of days between consecutive purchases per customer, I use LAG to bring the previous order date alongside each order, compute the day difference using DATEDIFF, and then average those gaps per customer. The first order for each customer has no previous order, so LAG returns NULL for that row, and I filter those NULL rows out before averaging so the first order does not distort the result.
keyPoints: LAG of order_date partitioned by customer ID and ordered by date gives each row access to the date of that customer's previous order.
DATEDIFF between the previous date and the current order date gives the number of days that passed between consecutive purchases.
Filtering out rows where the previous date is NULL removes the first order for each customer, since the first order has no predecessor and would otherwise contribute a NULL gap to the average.
Customers who placed only a single order will have only a NULL gap and will be excluded entirely from the result, which is correct since an average gap requires at least two orders.

code.snippet:
WITH cte AS (
    SELECT customer_id, order_date,
           LAG(order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_date
    FROM Orders
)
SELECT customer_id,
       AVG(DATEDIFF(DAY, prev_date, order_date)) AS avg_gap_days
FROM cte
WHERE prev_date IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY customer_id;
code.note: Exclude the first order's NULL gap

dialogue.text: Average days between purchases per customer.
LAG the previous order date, take DATEDIFF to the current, and AVG those gaps per customer, skipping the first order's NULL.
Why exclude prev_date IS NULL?
The first order has no predecessor, so its gap is NULL; including it is harmless for AVG but I filter it for clarity.

followUpsQA.a: They have only NULL gaps and drop out of the result.
PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY gap).

questions.q: Show the last purchase for each customer along with its order amount. Orders(customer_id, order_id, order_date, total_amount). (Asked at Google.)

answer.oneLiner: To retrieve the full details of each customer's last purchase, including the order amount, I use ROW_NUMBER partitioned by customer ID and ordered by order date descending, then filter to rows where the row number equals 1. This is more powerful than a simple GROUP BY with MAX of order date because it carries the entire row from the latest order, including the order ID and amount, rather than just the date.
keyPoints: ROW_NUMBER assigns a unique sequential number to each order within a customer's partition, ordered from newest to oldest.
Filtering to rows where the row number equals 1 keeps exactly the most recent order for each customer.
Unlike MAX of order_date with GROUP BY, the window approach retrieves the full row including all columns from that latest order, which is necessary when you need more than just the date.

If two orders share exactly the same latest date, adding a secondary sort criterion inside the ORDER BY of the window function, such as order_id descending, makes the result deterministic by picking one row consistently.

code.snippet:
WITH ranked_orders AS (
    SELECT customer_id, order_id, total_amount,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC) AS rn
    FROM Orders
)
SELECT customer_id, order_id, total_amount
FROM ranked_orders
WHERE rn = 1;
code.note: ROW_NUMBER keeps the full latest row

dialogue.text: Each customer's last purchase with its amount.
ROW_NUMBER per customer ordered by date desc, then keep rn = 1, which carries the amount along.
Why not GROUP BY with MAX(order_date)?
MAX gives the date but not the amount from that row; the window approach preserves the whole latest record.

followUpsQA.a: Add a tiebreaker like order_id DESC inside the ORDER BY for determinism.
ROW_NUMBER guarantees one row; RANK could keep multiple on a tie.

questions.q: Calculate year-over-year (YoY) growth in revenue. Orders(order_date, total_amount). (Asked at Microsoft.)

answer.oneLiner: Year-over-year revenue growth is computed by first aggregating total revenue per year, then using LAG to place the previous year's revenue alongside each year's revenue in the same row, and finally subtracting to get the difference. The first year in the data has no prior year, so LAG returns NULL for that row and the growth column will be NULL, which is the correct and expected behavior.
keyPoints: A derived table or CTE groups orders by YEAR of order date and sums the amounts to get one revenue figure per year.
LAG of the revenue column ordered by year brings the previous year's revenue into the current row so the subtraction can happen in a single expression.
The year-over-year change is current revenue minus the previous year's revenue; a positive value means growth and a negative value means decline.
To express growth as a percentage, divide the difference by the previous year's revenue and multiply by 100, but add a guard against dividing by zero or NULL when the previous year has no data.

code.snippet:
SELECT yr,
       revenue,
       revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY yr) AS yoy_growth
FROM (
    SELECT YEAR(order_date) AS yr, SUM(total_amount) AS revenue
    FROM Orders
    GROUP BY YEAR(order_date)
) t
ORDER BY yr;
code.note: LAG over yearly totals

dialogue.text: Year-over-year revenue growth.
I total revenue per year, then LAG the previous year's value and subtract; the first year is NULL with no prior.
Express it as a percentage?
(revenue - prev) * 100.0 / prev, guarding against a zero or NULL prior year.

followUpsQA.a: LAG needs one row per year; otherwise it compares individual orders.
LAG compares to the previous present year; a calendar table fills true gaps.

questions.q: Detect customers whose order amount is higher than their own historical 90th percentile. Orders(customer_id, order_id, total_amount). (Asked at Amazon.)

answer.oneLiner: To find orders above each customer's 90th percentile, I use NTILE to divide each customer's orders into ten equal buckets ranked by amount, with the highest amounts in bucket 10, and then I keep only bucket 10. NTILE is an approximation of the percentile because it splits by row count, so a customer with only a few orders will have unevenly sized buckets. If you need the exact 90th percentile cutoff, you would use PERCENTILE_CONT instead.
keyPoints: NTILE of 10 partitioned by customer ID and ordered by total amount ascending divides each customer's orders into ten roughly equal groups, with the top 10 percent of orders landing in bucket 10.
Filtering to decile 10 returns the orders in the top tenth of each customer's spending distribution, which approximates orders above the 90th percentile.

NTILE is an approximation because it splits rows evenly by count; for a customer with only three orders, bucket 10 gets just one row, and the boundaries between buckets may not correspond to true percentile values.

The exact alternative is PERCENTILE_CONT of 0.9 WITHIN GROUP ordered by total amount, computed as a window function over the customer partition, which interpolates the true 90th percentile value and lets you compare each order directly against it.

code.snippet:
WITH ranked_orders AS (
    SELECT customer_id, order_id, total_amount,
           NTILE(10) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY total_amount) AS decile
    FROM Orders
)
SELECT customer_id, order_id, total_amount
FROM ranked_orders
WHERE decile = 10;
code.note: Top decile per customer via NTILE(10)

dialogue.text: Orders above each customer's 90th percentile.
I NTILE the customer's orders into 10 buckets by amount and keep decile 10 — the top tenth.
Is NTILE exactly the 90th percentile?
Not exactly; NTILE splits by row count and is approximate with few orders. For precision I'd compare against PERCENTILE_CONT(0.9) per customer.

followUpsQA.a: Compute PERCENTILE_CONT(0.9) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY total_amount) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id) and compare.
When a customer has very few orders, buckets are uneven.

questions.q: Retrieve the longest gap (in days) between consecutive orders for each customer. Orders(customer_id, order_date). (Asked at Amazon.)

answer.oneLiner: To find the longest gap between orders for each customer, I use LAG to get each order's previous date, compute the day difference with DATEDIFF, and then aggregate with MAX per customer to find the largest of those gaps. Rows where the previous date is NULL represent each customer's first order and are filtered out before taking the MAX.
keyPoints: LAG of order_date partitioned by customer and ordered chronologically gives each row the date of the previous order for that customer.
DATEDIFF between the previous date and the current date gives the number of days that elapsed between two consecutive purchases.
Filtering out rows where the previous date is NULL removes each customer's first order, which has no predecessor and therefore no meaningful gap.
MAX of the day differences per customer collapses all the individual gaps into a single longest gap value for each customer.

code.snippet:
WITH cte AS (
    SELECT customer_id, order_date,
           LAG(order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_order_date
    FROM Orders
)
SELECT customer_id,
       MAX(DATEDIFF(DAY, prev_order_date, order_date)) AS max_gap
FROM cte
WHERE prev_order_date IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY customer_id;
code.note: MAX of consecutive day gaps

dialogue.text: Longest gap between orders per customer.
LAG the previous date, DATEDIFF to the current, and take the MAX gap per customer, ignoring the first order's NULL.
Single-order customers?
They only have a NULL gap, so they're filtered out; I could LEFT JOIN them back as NULL/0 if needed.

followUpsQA.q: Why MAX after the per-row gaps?
followUpsQA.a: Each row holds one gap; MAX collapses them to the longest per customer.

followUpsQA.q: Also return when the gap occurred?
followUpsQA.a: Keep the row achieving the MAX via a top-1-per-customer window.

questions.q: Identify customers with total revenue below the 10th percentile. Orders(customer_id, total_amount). (Asked at Google.)

answer.oneLiner: To find customers in the bottom 10 percent by total revenue, I first sum their order amounts per customer in a CTE, and then compute the 10th percentile cutoff value using PERCENTILE_CONT. In SQL Server, PERCENTILE_CONT is a window function that requires an OVER clause and returns the interpolated value on every row, so I take a single DISTINCT value from it to use as a scalar threshold and then filter customers whose total is below that threshold.
keyPoints: The CTE uses SUM of total amount grouped by customer ID to compute each customer's lifetime revenue.

PERCENTILE_CONT of 0.1 WITHIN GROUP ordered by total revenue OVER an empty window computes the 10th percentile cutoff as an interpolated value, meaning it calculates a value that would fall at the 10th percentile point even if that exact value does not exist in the data.
In SQL Server, PERCENTILE_CONT is a window function rather than an aggregate, so it returns the same cutoff value on every row of the result and you need to take DISTINCT to get a single scalar.

PERCENTILE_DISC is similar to PERCENTILE_CONT but instead of interpolating it returns the actual existing data value closest to the target percentile, so the two functions can give different answers when data is sparse.

code.snippet:
WITH cte AS (
    SELECT customer_id, SUM(total_amount) AS total_revenue
    FROM Orders
    GROUP BY customer_id
),
p AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.1) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY total_revenue) OVER () AS p10
    FROM cte
)
SELECT c.customer_id, c.total_revenue
FROM cte c
CROSS JOIN p
WHERE c.total_revenue < p.p10;
code.note: PERCENTILE_CONT is a window function in T-SQL (needs OVER ())

dialogue.text: Customers in the bottom 10% by total revenue.
I total revenue per customer, compute the 10th-percentile cutoff with PERCENTILE_CONT, and keep customers below it.
Why the OVER () and DISTINCT around PERCENTILE_CONT?
In SQL Server PERCENTILE_CONT is a window function returning the value on every row, so I take a single DISTINCT value to use as a scalar cutoff.

followUpsQA.a: CONT interpolates between values; DISC returns an actual existing value.
Postgres exposes percentile_cont as a plain ordered-set aggregate usable in a scalar subquery.

questions.q: Recyclable and Low Fat Products. Products(product_id PK, low_fats ENUM('Y','N'), recyclable ENUM('Y','N')). Find the ids of products that are both low fat and recyclable.

answer.oneLiner: This is the simplest kind of SQL filter: two equality conditions joined by AND so that only rows satisfying both conditions are returned. I select the product_id and filter where low_fats equals Y and recyclable equals Y, and because product_id is the primary key I do not need DISTINCT.

code.note: Basic WHERE with AND

keyPoints: The AND operator means both conditions must be true simultaneously for a row to qualify, so a product that is recyclable but not low fat would be excluded.
Because product_id is the primary key, each row is already unique and adding DISTINCT would have no effect.
If the question asked for products that are either low fat or recyclable, I would use OR instead, but the word both in the problem description requires AND.

dialogue.text: Find product ids that are both low fat and recyclable.
I'd SELECT product_id and filter WHERE low_fats = 'Y' AND recyclable = 'Y'.
Why AND and not OR?
Both conditions must hold simultaneously, so AND is required; OR would return products meeting only one criterion.
Do you need DISTINCT here?
No, product_id is the primary key so each row is already unique.

followUpsQA.a: Both flags must be 'Y' at once.
No, product_id is the primary key.

followUpsQA.q: Would an index help this filter?
followUpsQA.a: A composite index on (low_fats, recyclable) could, though tables are tiny here.

questions.q: Find Customer Referee. Customer(id PK, name, referee_id). Report the names of customers that are NOT referred by the customer with id = 2 (note: referee_id can be NULL).

answer.oneLiner: The tricky part of this question is handling NULL values in the referee_id column. A plain not-equal comparison like referee_id not equal to 2 in SQL returns UNKNOWN rather than TRUE when referee_id is NULL, so those rows are silently excluded from the result. To include customers with no referee at all, I add an explicit OR IS NULL check.

code.note: NULL-aware filtering: <> never matches NULL

keyPoints: In SQL, any comparison involving NULL evaluates to UNKNOWN rather than TRUE or FALSE, which means NULL not equal to 2 is not considered true and the row is filtered out.
Adding OR referee_id IS NULL to the WHERE clause explicitly includes customers who have no referee, which is the correct interpretation of not being referred by customer 2.

An alternative is to use COALESCE to substitute a safe placeholder value for NULL before comparing, such as COALESCE of referee_id and 0 not equal to 2, which achieves the same result in a single predicate.

dialogue.text: Report customers not referred by customer id 2. Note referee_id can be NULL.
I'd filter WHERE referee_id <> 2, but that alone drops NULLs, so I add OR referee_id IS NULL.
Why does <> 2 drop the NULL rows?
In SQL, NULL <> 2 evaluates to UNKNOWN, not TRUE, so those rows are filtered out unless I handle NULL explicitly.
Is there a cleaner way to express that?
Yes, COALESCE(referee_id, 0) <> 2 collapses the NULL handling into one predicate.

followUpsQA.a: NULL <> 2 is UNKNOWN, which fails the filter.
WHERE COALESCE(referee_id, 0) <> 2.
No, NOT IN with comparisons to NULL also yields UNKNOWN.

questions.q: Big Countries. World(name PK, continent, area, population, gdp). A country is big if area >= 3000000 OR population >= 25000000. Return name, population, area.

answer.oneLiner: A country is big if it meets either of two criteria: large area or large population. Since either condition alone qualifies the country, I use OR to connect the two predicates in the WHERE clause. I select only the three columns the question asks for: name, population, and area.

code.note: OR condition on two thresholds

keyPoints: OR means a row qualifies if at least one of the conditions is true, so a country with a huge population but a small area would still be selected.
Using AND instead of OR would require a country to be both large in area and large in population, which would be too restrictive and exclude valid results.
An OR filter on two different columns usually cannot take advantage of a single composite index, so the database may perform a table scan or use an index-merge strategy on large tables.

dialogue.text: Find big countries where area >= 3000000 OR population >= 25000000.
I'd SELECT name, population, area and filter WHERE area >= 3000000 OR population >= 25000000.
Why OR rather than AND?
The definition says a country qualifies if either threshold is met, so AND would be too strict.
Could an index speed this up?
An OR across two columns usually can't use a single index well; the engine may scan or do an index merge.

followUpsQA.a: Either threshold alone makes a country big.

followUpsQA.q: Can an index help an OR on two columns?
followUpsQA.a: Often not a single index; an index-merge or scan is common.

followUpsQA.q: How would you rewrite OR as UNION?
followUpsQA.a: Two SELECTs (one per condition) combined with UNION to dedupe.

questions.q: Article Views I. Views(article_id, author_id, viewer_id, view_date). Find all authors who viewed at least one of their own articles. Return the distinct ids sorted ascending.

answer.oneLiner: An author viewing their own article means the author_id and viewer_id columns in the same row are equal. I filter to those rows, select DISTINCT author_id to avoid listing the same author multiple times if they viewed several of their own articles, and order the result ascending as required.

code.snippet:
SELECT DISTINCT author_id AS id
FROM Views
WHERE author_id = viewer_id
ORDER BY id;
code.note: DISTINCT + equality predicate

keyPoints: The condition author_id equal to viewer_id identifies rows where the person who wrote the article is also the person who viewed it, which is what self-view means.
DISTINCT is necessary because an author might self-view several articles or view the same article multiple times, which would produce duplicate author IDs without the deduplication.
Ordering by the aliased id column ascending satisfies the output ordering requirement, and the alias is referenced directly in the ORDER BY clause.

dialogue.text: Find authors who viewed at least one of their own articles, distinct ids ascending.
I'd filter WHERE author_id = viewer_id, then SELECT DISTINCT author_id and ORDER BY it ascending.
Why DISTINCT?
An author may self-view many articles or the same one repeatedly, so DISTINCT collapses those to one id.
Does the ORDER BY work after the alias?
Yes, I alias author_id AS id and ORDER BY id, which the engine resolves to the selected column.

followUpsQA.a: An author can self-view multiple times, producing duplicate ids.
Yes, GROUP BY author_id gives the same de-duplicated result.
The author viewed their own article.

questions.q: Invalid Tweets. Tweets(tweet_id PK, content). Find the ids of invalid tweets, where a tweet is invalid if the number of characters in content is strictly greater than 15.

answer.oneLiner: I filter tweets where the character length of the content column is strictly greater than 15, which identifies the invalid ones. I use CHAR_LENGTH rather than LENGTH because LENGTH counts bytes in MySQL, and a multibyte character like an emoji would inflate the byte count beyond the actual number of characters, producing incorrect results.

code.note: CHAR_LENGTH counts characters (not bytes like LENGTH)

keyPoints: CHAR_LENGTH counts the number of characters in a string regardless of how many bytes each character takes, making it safe for multibyte encodings like UTF-8.
LENGTH in MySQL counts the number of bytes, so a two-byte character would count as 2 rather than 1 and could incorrectly classify a tweet as invalid.
The threshold is strictly greater than 15, so a tweet with exactly 15 characters is still valid and should not appear in the results.
The SQL Server equivalent is LEN, which counts characters rather than bytes.

dialogue.text: Find invalid tweets where content has strictly more than 15 characters.
I'd SELECT tweet_id and filter WHERE CHAR_LENGTH(content) > 15.
Why CHAR_LENGTH instead of LENGTH?
LENGTH counts bytes, so a multibyte character would inflate the count; CHAR_LENGTH counts actual characters, which is what 'characters' means here.
Strictly greater than, any boundary concern?
Exactly 15 is valid, so I use > 15, not >= 15.

followUpsQA.a: LENGTH counts bytes; CHAR_LENGTH counts characters, safe for multibyte.
Exactly 15 characters is still valid.
LEN(content) > 15.

questions.q: Replace Employee ID With The Unique Identifier. Employees(id PK, name), EmployeeUNI(id, unique_id). Show the unique_id of each user; if a user has no unique_id, show NULL.

answer.oneLiner: I use a LEFT JOIN starting from the Employees table so that every employee is preserved in the result, even those who have no entry in the EmployeeUNI table. For employees without a unique ID, the join produces NULL for the unique_id column, which is exactly the output required.

code.note: LEFT JOIN preserves rows without a match

keyPoints: A LEFT JOIN keeps all rows from the left table, Employees in this case, regardless of whether a matching row exists in the right table EmployeeUNI.
When no match exists in EmployeeUNI for a given employee, the joined columns from that table are filled with NULL, which naturally produces the required NULL output for employees without a unique ID.
Using an INNER JOIN instead would silently drop employees who have no unique ID entry, which would be wrong.

dialogue.text: Show each user's unique_id, NULL if they have none.
I'd LEFT JOIN Employees to EmployeeUNI on id so every employee is preserved even without a match.
What happens to employees with no unique_id row?
The LEFT JOIN produces NULL for eu.unique_id on those rows, which is exactly what's asked.
Would an INNER JOIN work?
No, INNER JOIN would silently drop employees who lack a unique_id.

followUpsQA.q: Why LEFT JOIN over INNER JOIN?
followUpsQA.a: LEFT keeps employees without a unique_id; INNER would drop them.
Employees, on the left, so all employees survive.

followUpsQA.q: What value appears for an unmatched row?
followUpsQA.a: NULL for unique_id.

questions.q: Product Sales Analysis I. Sales(sale_id, product_id, year, quantity, price), Product(product_id PK, product_name). Report product_name, year, and price for each Sales row.

answer.oneLiner: This is a basic inner join that enriches each sales row with the product name from the Product table. Every sales row references a product via the product_id foreign key, and because that key is guaranteed to exist in the Product table, an inner join is appropriate here and will not drop any rows.

code.note: Simple INNER JOIN on FK

keyPoints: Joining Sales to Product on product_id pulls the product name into each sales row so it can be included in the output.
An INNER JOIN is safe here because every sale has a valid product_id that exists in the Product table, so no rows are lost.
Because product_id is the primary key of Product, each sales row matches exactly one product row and the join does not multiply rows.

dialogue.text: Report product_name, year, and price for each Sales row.
I'd INNER JOIN Sales to Product on product_id and project the three requested columns.
Why is an INNER JOIN safe here?
product_id is a foreign key into Product, so every sale has a matching product; nothing is dropped.
Do you risk duplicate rows?
No, Product.product_id is the primary key, so each sale matches exactly one product row.

followUpsQA.q: Why is INNER JOIN safe here?
followUpsQA.a: Every sale has a matching product via the FK.
No, product_id is unique in Product.

followUpsQA.q: When would you need a LEFT JOIN instead?
followUpsQA.a: If you wanted sales even when the product row is missing.

questions.q: Customer Who Visited but Did Not Make Any Transactions. Visits(visit_id, customer_id), Transactions(transaction_id, visit_id, amount). For each customer, find how many times they visited without making a transaction. Return customer_id, count_no_trans.

answer.oneLiner: I LEFT JOIN the Visits table to the Transactions table on visit_id, which keeps every visit including ones with no transactions. Rows where transaction_id is NULL after the join are visits where the customer browsed but did not buy anything, and counting those per customer gives the answer.

code.snippet:
SELECT v.customer_id, COUNT(*) AS count_no_trans
FROM Visits v
LEFT JOIN Transactions t ON v.visit_id = t.visit_id
WHERE t.transaction_id IS NULL
GROUP BY v.customer_id;
code.note: LEFT JOIN + IS NULL = anti-join

keyPoints: A LEFT JOIN from Visits to Transactions on visit_id preserves every visit row regardless of whether a transaction exists for that visit.
After the join, rows where the transaction_id column from Transactions is NULL represent visits with no transactions, which is the classic anti-join pattern.
GROUP BY customer_id with COUNT of all such NULL-transaction rows gives the number of transaction-free visits per customer.

dialogue.text: For each customer, count visits where they made no transaction.
I'd LEFT JOIN Visits to Transactions on visit_id, then keep only rows where transaction_id IS NULL.
Why does IS NULL isolate the right rows?
A visit with no transaction has no match, so the joined transaction columns are NULL; that's the classic anti-join pattern.
How do you produce the per-customer count?
GROUP BY customer_id and COUNT(*) over the surviving null-transaction visits.

followUpsQA.q: What pattern is LEFT JOIN + IS NULL?
followUpsQA.a: An anti-join, finding rows with no match.

followUpsQA.q: Why count after filtering IS NULL?
followUpsQA.a: Only no-transaction visits should be counted.
Yes, NOT EXISTS on a correlated transaction subquery is equivalent.

questions.q: Rising Temperature. Weather(id PK, recordDate, temperature). Find all dates' ids with a higher temperature than the previous (yesterday's) date.

answer.oneLiner: I self-join the Weather table to itself using two aliases and connect them where the day difference is exactly 1, meaning one row represents today and the other represents yesterday. Then I filter to pairs where today's temperature is higher than yesterday's to find the warming days.

code.snippet:
SELECT w1.id
FROM Weather w1
JOIN Weather w2
  ON DATEDIFF(w1.recordDate, w2.recordDate) = 1
WHERE w1.temperature > w2.temperature;
code.note: Self-join on consecutive days via DATEDIFF

keyPoints: A self-join connects a table to itself using two aliases, letting you compare rows that belong to different dates within the same table.
Using DATEDIFF to test that the date difference equals exactly 1 is more reliable than subtracting ID values, because dates can have gaps and IDs are not guaranteed to be consecutive.
The temperature comparison in the WHERE clause ensures only days that are genuinely warmer than the previous day appear in the result.
An alternative is to use LAG of temperature over the window ordered by date and compare the current row's temperature to the prior row's temperature, which avoids the self-join entirely.

dialogue.text: Find ids of days warmer than the previous day.
I'd self-join Weather to itself where DATEDIFF(w1.date, w2.date) = 1 and w1.temperature > w2.temperature.
Why DATEDIFF rather than subtracting one from the id?
Dates may have gaps and ids don't guarantee consecutive days, so DATEDIFF = 1 reliably ties yesterday to today.
Any modern alternative to the self-join?
LAG(temperature) OVER (ORDER BY recordDate) lets me compare to the prior row without a join.

followUpsQA.q: Why DATEDIFF instead of id arithmetic?
followUpsQA.a: Dates can have gaps; ids aren't guaranteed consecutive.
Compare temperature to LAG(temperature) ordered by date.
It pairs each day with the prior day's row.

questions.q: Average Time of Process per Machine. Activity(machine_id, process_id, activity_type ENUM('start','end'), timestamp). For each machine compute the average time to complete a process (end timestamp minus start timestamp), rounded to 3 decimals.

answer.oneLiner: To compute each machine's average process time, I self-join the Activity table on machine_id and process_id, pairing start rows with their corresponding end rows for the same process. I compute the time difference by subtracting the start timestamp from the end timestamp, average those differences per machine, and round to three decimal places.

code.snippet:
SELECT a.machine_id,
       ROUND(AVG(b.timestamp - a.timestamp), 3) AS processing_time
FROM Activity a
JOIN Activity b
  ON a.machine_id = b.machine_id
 AND a.process_id = b.process_id
 AND a.activity_type = 'start'
 AND b.activity_type = 'end'
GROUP BY a.machine_id;
code.note: Self-join pairing start/end, then AVG + ROUND

keyPoints: The self-join connects each start activity row to the matching end activity row for the same machine and process, using the activity_type column to distinguish start from end.
Specifying that alias a must have activity_type equal to start and alias b must have activity_type equal to end ensures the subtraction always goes in the right direction, giving a positive duration.
AVG over those differences per machine_id computes the mean processing time across all processes on that machine.

An alternative without the self-join is conditional aggregation: compute the average of end timestamps minus the average of start timestamps per machine, which works when each process has exactly one start and one end.

dialogue.text: Compute each machine's average process time from start/end activity rows.
I'd self-join Activity to itself on machine_id and process_id, pairing the 'start' row with the 'end' row, then average (end - start).
How do you ensure you subtract end minus start, not the reverse?
I pin a.activity_type = 'start' and b.activity_type = 'end' in the join, so b.timestamp - a.timestamp is always positive.
Final shaping?
GROUP BY machine_id and wrap AVG in ROUND(..., 3) for three decimals.

followUpsQA.q: How do you pair start with end?
followUpsQA.a: Self-join on machine_id and process_id with the type predicates.

followUpsQA.q: How do you guarantee a positive duration?
followUpsQA.a: Pin a as 'start', b as 'end', so b - a is positive.

followUpsQA.q: Could you do it without a self-join?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, conditional aggregation: AVG(end_ts) - AVG(start_ts) per machine.

questions.q: Employee Bonus. Employee(empId PK, name, supervisor, salary), Bonus(empId, bonus). Report the name and bonus of employees with a bonus less than 1000, including employees who have no bonus record.

answer.oneLiner: Employees with no bonus have no row in the Bonus table, so I need a LEFT JOIN to keep them in the result. After the join, employees with no bonus have NULL for the bonus column, and NULL is less than 1000 evaluates to UNKNOWN in SQL, so I must explicitly include an OR IS NULL check to make sure no-bonus employees are not accidentally filtered out.

code.snippet:
SELECT e.name, b.bonus
FROM Employee e
LEFT JOIN Bonus b ON e.empId = b.empId
WHERE b.bonus < 1000 OR b.bonus IS NULL;
code.note: LEFT JOIN; IS NULL captures employees with no bonus

keyPoints: A LEFT JOIN from Employee to Bonus on empId keeps all employees in the result, including those who have no bonus entry at all.
For employees with no bonus row, the bonus column after the LEFT JOIN contains NULL, and NULL compared to any number returns UNKNOWN rather than TRUE.
Adding OR bonus IS NULL to the filter explicitly includes employees with no bonus, because the NULL comparison alone would exclude them.
An alternative is to use COALESCE to replace NULL with zero before comparing, writing COALESCE of bonus and zero less than 1000, which is a single cleaner predicate.

dialogue.text: Report name and bonus for employees with bonus under 1000, including those with no bonus.
I'd LEFT JOIN Employee to Bonus on empId, then filter WHERE b.bonus < 1000 OR b.bonus IS NULL.
Why the explicit IS NULL?
Employees with no bonus row get NULL, and NULL < 1000 is UNKNOWN, so they'd be dropped without the OR IS NULL.
Could you avoid the OR?
Yes, COALESCE(b.bonus, 0) < 1000 folds the no-bonus case into one comparison.

followUpsQA.q: Why include OR bonus IS NULL?
followUpsQA.a: NULL < 1000 is UNKNOWN, so no-bonus employees would be excluded.
WHERE COALESCE(b.bonus, 0) < 1000.
To keep employees who have no bonus record.

questions.q: Students and Examinations. Students(student_id, student_name), Subjects(subject_name), Examinations(student_id, subject_name). For every student and every subject, count how many exams the student took. Order by student_id, then subject_name.

answer.oneLiner: The challenge here is that we need to show a count of zero for combinations where a student never took an exam in a subject. To achieve that, I CROSS JOIN Students with Subjects to generate every possible student-subject pair, then LEFT JOIN that result to the Examinations table to attach actual exam records. COUNT of a column from the Examinations side returns zero for pairs with no matching rows.

code.snippet:
SELECT s.student_id, s.student_name, sub.subject_name,
       COUNT(e.subject_name) AS attended_exams
FROM Students s
CROSS JOIN Subjects sub
LEFT JOIN Examinations e
  ON e.student_id = s.student_id
 AND e.subject_name = sub.subject_name
GROUP BY s.student_id, s.student_name, sub.subject_name
ORDER BY s.student_id, sub.subject_name;
code.note: CROSS JOIN builds all pairs; COUNT(col) ignores NULLs

keyPoints: A CROSS JOIN between Students and Subjects produces every possible combination of a student and a subject, which is necessary so that pairs with zero exams still appear in the result.
LEFT JOIN to Examinations attaches exam rows where they exist while preserving all student-subject pairs that have no matching exam.

COUNT of a specific column from the Examinations table, rather than COUNT of all rows, counts only non-NULL values and therefore returns zero for student-subject pairs that had no exams after the LEFT JOIN.
GROUP BY student_id, student_name, and subject_name collapses all exam rows for each pair into a single count.

dialogue.text: For every student and every subject, count exams taken, even zero.
I'd CROSS JOIN Students with Subjects to get all pairs, LEFT JOIN Examinations, then COUNT per pair.
Why CROSS JOIN first?
It guarantees a row for every student-subject combination, including ones the student never sat, so we can report zeros.
How do untaken subjects become 0 rather than dropped?
COUNT(e.subject_name) ignores the NULLs from the LEFT JOIN, yielding 0 for combinations with no exam.

followUpsQA.a: To create every student-subject pair so zeros can appear.
COUNT(column) skips NULLs, giving 0 for untaken subjects.

followUpsQA.q: What does the LEFT JOIN add?
followUpsQA.a: It attaches actual exams while keeping all pairs.

questions.q: Managers with at Least 5 Direct Reports. Employee(id PK, name, department, managerId). Find managers who have at least five direct reports. Return their names.

answer.oneLiner: To find managers with at least five direct reports, I group the Employee table by manager_id and use HAVING to keep only those manager IDs with a count of five or more. Then I look up those manager IDs in the Employee table to retrieve their names, since the employee who is a manager also has a row in the same table with their name.

code.snippet:
SELECT name
FROM Employee
WHERE id IN (
    SELECT managerId
    FROM Employee
    GROUP BY managerId
    HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5
);
code.note: GROUP BY ... HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5, then map id back to name

keyPoints: Grouping by manager_id and counting the rows in each group gives you the number of direct reports for each manager.
HAVING is required instead of WHERE because you are filtering on an aggregated count, which does not exist until after the grouping step.
The manager IDs that pass the HAVING filter are then used to look up names in the Employee table using a WHERE IN subquery or a self-join, since each manager is also an employee with their own row.
Managers with zero direct reports do not appear in this query because they have no subordinate rows that reference their ID as manager_id.

dialogue.text: Find managers with at least five direct reports; return their names.
I'd GROUP BY managerId and keep groups with COUNT(*) >= 5 via HAVING, then map those ids back to names.
Why HAVING instead of WHERE?
WHERE filters rows before aggregation; the count is an aggregate, so it must be filtered with HAVING after grouping.
How do you turn manager ids into names?
I feed the grouped ids into a subquery and select names WHERE id IN that set, effectively a self-reference on Employee.

followUpsQA.a: Aggregates like COUNT can only be filtered after grouping, with HAVING.

followUpsQA.q: How do you get the manager's name?
followUpsQA.a: Map the grouped ids back via id IN (subquery) or a self-join.

followUpsQA.q: What if managerId can be NULL?
followUpsQA.a: NULL groups together but won't match a real id, so it's harmless here.

questions.q: Confirmation Rate. Signups(user_id PK, time_stamp), Confirmations(user_id, time_stamp, action ENUM('confirmed','timeout')). The confirmation rate of a user is the number of 'confirmed' messages divided by total requests. Report user_id and confirmation_rate rounded to 2 decimals (0 if no requests).

answer.oneLiner: I LEFT JOIN the Signups table to the Confirmations table so that users who never sent a confirmation request still appear with a null confirmation record. Then I compute the confirmation rate by averaging the boolean expression that checks whether each action is confirmed, which gives the fraction of confirmed messages, and I wrap that in IFNULL to return zero for users who have no rows in the Confirmations table at all.

code.snippet:
SELECT s.user_id,
       ROUND(IFNULL(AVG(c.action = 'confirmed'), 0), 2) AS confirmation_rate
FROM Signups s
LEFT JOIN Confirmations c ON s.user_id = c.user_id
GROUP BY s.user_id;
code.note: AVG of a boolean = fraction true; IFNULL handles no-request users

keyPoints: A LEFT JOIN from Signups to Confirmations keeps every user in the result, even those who never requested a confirmation.

In MySQL, a boolean expression like action equal to confirmed evaluates to 1 when true and 0 when false, so averaging those values across all confirmation rows for a user gives the fraction that were confirmed.
For users with no confirmation rows at all, the AVG returns NULL because it is averaging over an empty set, and IFNULL converts that to zero as required by the problem.
ROUND to two decimal places is applied as the final step to format the confirmation rate correctly.

dialogue.text: Compute each user's confirmation rate = confirmed / total requests, 0 if none.
I'd LEFT JOIN Signups to Confirmations and take AVG(c.action = 'confirmed'), which averages 1s and 0s into the fraction.
What about users with no requests at all?
Their AVG is NULL, so I wrap it in IFNULL(..., 0) and ROUND to two decimals.
Why does AVG of a boolean give the rate?
In MySQL a boolean evaluates to 1 or 0, so its average is exactly the proportion that are true.

followUpsQA.q: Why does AVG of a boolean give the rate?
followUpsQA.a: True/false become 1/0, so the average is the fraction true.
Users with no requests have NULL average; default to 0.
To keep users who never had a confirmation row.

questions.q: Not Boring Movies. Cinema(id PK, movie, description, rating). Report movies with an odd id and a description that is not 'boring', ordered by rating descending.

answer.oneLiner: I filter to movies with an odd id using the modulo operator and exclude those whose description is exactly the word boring. Then I order the qualifying movies by rating descending so the highest-rated ones appear first.

code.snippet:
SELECT *
FROM Cinema
WHERE id % 2 = 1 AND description <> 'boring'
ORDER BY rating DESC;
code.note: Modulo for odd ids; ORDER BY DESC

keyPoints: The modulo operator id percent 2 equal to 1 selects rows where the id is odd because odd numbers leave a remainder of 1 when divided by 2.
The description not equal to boring condition uses a case-sensitive or case-insensitive comparison depending on the database collation, but for this problem the data is straightforward.
ORDER BY rating descending sorts the results from the highest rating to the lowest, which is what the problem requires.

dialogue.text: Report movies with an odd id and description not 'boring', ordered by rating descending.
I'd filter WHERE id % 2 = 1 AND description <> 'boring', then ORDER BY rating DESC.
How are you testing for an odd id?
id % 2 = 1 picks odd ids; MOD(id, 2) = 1 is the portable equivalent.
Does <> 'boring' exclude any rows it shouldn't?
Only exact 'boring' descriptions are excluded; here description is non-NULL so the comparison is straightforward.

followUpsQA.q: How do you select odd ids?
followUpsQA.a: id % 2 = 1, or MOD(id, 2) = 1.

followUpsQA.q: What if description could be NULL?
followUpsQA.a: Add OR description IS NULL since NULL <> 'boring' is UNKNOWN.
After the WHERE clause, sorting the filtered rows.

questions.q: Average Selling Price. Prices(product_id, start_date, end_date, price), UnitsSold(product_id, purchase_date, units). For each product compute the average selling price = SUM(price*units)/SUM(units), rounded to 2 decimals; products with no sales get 0.

answer.oneLiner: The average selling price is a weighted average where each period's price is weighted by the number of units sold during that period. I join the Prices table to UnitsSold using a date range condition so that each unit's sale is matched to whichever price window was active on its purchase date, then compute SUM of price times units divided by SUM of units per product.

code.snippet:
SELECT p.product_id,
       IFNULL(ROUND(SUM(p.price * u.units) / SUM(u.units), 2), 0) AS average_price
FROM Prices p
LEFT JOIN UnitsSold u
  ON p.product_id = u.product_id
 AND u.purchase_date BETWEEN p.start_date AND p.end_date
GROUP BY p.product_id;
code.note: Date-range join + weighted average; IFNULL for no-sales (division by NULL)

keyPoints: The join condition includes BETWEEN start_date AND end_date so that a unit sold on a given date is matched only to the price that was valid on that date, not to all historical prices.
The weighted average formula is SUM of price multiplied by units divided by SUM of units, which weights each price by how many units were sold at that price.
For products with no sales, the division produces NULL because SUM of units is NULL over an empty set, and IFNULL replaces that with zero as required.
Using a LEFT JOIN from Prices to UnitsSold means that price windows with no matching sales are still preserved and contribute to the result correctly.

dialogue.text: Compute each product's average selling price = SUM(price*units)/SUM(units), 0 if no sales.
I'd LEFT JOIN Prices to UnitsSold where purchase_date falls BETWEEN start_date and end_date, then take the weighted average.
Why a date-range join condition?
Each price is valid for a window, so a unit's price depends on which window its purchase_date falls into.
What about products with no sales?
SUM(units) is NULL there, so the division is NULL; I wrap it in IFNULL(..., 0).

followUpsQA.q: Why join on a date range?
followUpsQA.a: Price is only valid within its start/end window.
No-sales products divide by NULL, defaulting to 0.

followUpsQA.q: Why weighted average not plain AVG?
followUpsQA.a: Different quantities sold must weight each price.

questions.q: Project Employees I. Project(project_id, employee_id), Employee(employee_id PK, name, experience_years). For each project report the average experience years of its employees, rounded to 2 decimals.

answer.oneLiner: I join the Project table to the Employee table on employee_id so I can access each employee's experience years, then group by project_id and take the average experience years across all employees on each project. Wrapping the average in ROUND to two decimal places formats the output as the problem requires.

code.snippet:
SELECT p.project_id,
       ROUND(AVG(e.experience_years), 2) AS average_years
FROM Project p
JOIN Employee e ON p.employee_id = e.employee_id
GROUP BY p.project_id;
code.note: JOIN + AVG + ROUND grouped by project

keyPoints: Joining Project to Employee on employee_id enriches each project-employee assignment row with the employee's experience_years value.
GROUP BY project_id collapses all employee rows for each project into a single aggregate row.
AVG of experience_years computes the mean number of years of experience across all employees assigned to each project.
An INNER JOIN is appropriate here because every project-employee row should have a corresponding employee record.

dialogue.text: For each project, report the average experience years of its employees.
I'd JOIN Project to Employee on employee_id, GROUP BY project_id, and AVG(experience_years).
Why an inner join is fine here?
Every project row references a real employee, so there's nothing to preserve with a LEFT JOIN.
How do you get two decimals?
Wrap the AVG in ROUND(..., 2).

followUpsQA.a: To average experience within each project.
The problem requires two decimal places.
If a project could list a non-existent employee.

questions.q: Percentage of Users Attended a Contest. Users(user_id PK, user_name), Register(contest_id, user_id). For each contest, report the percentage of all users who registered, rounded to 2 decimals. Order by percentage descending, then contest_id ascending.

answer.oneLiner: To compute the percentage of all users who registered for each contest, I group the Register table by contest_id, count the distinct users who registered, and divide by the total user count from a scalar subquery. The scalar subquery retrieves the total number of users from the Users table once and that value is reused for every contest in the result.

code.snippet:
SELECT contest_id,
       ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) * 100.0 / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Users), 2) AS percentage
FROM Register
GROUP BY contest_id
ORDER BY percentage DESC, contest_id ASC;
code.note: Scalar subquery for the denominator (total users)

keyPoints: COUNT of DISTINCT user_id per contest gives the number of unique users who registered for that contest, accounting for any potential duplicate entries.
Dividing by a scalar subquery that counts all rows in the Users table gives the fraction of all users who registered, and multiplying by 100.0 converts it to a percentage.
The ORDER BY clause sorts first by percentage descending so the most popular contests appear first, with contest_id ascending as a tiebreaker.
Multiplying by 100.0 rather than 100 ensures floating point arithmetic is used so the decimal portion of the percentage is preserved.

dialogue.text: For each contest, report the percentage of all users who registered.
I'd GROUP BY contest_id and compute COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) * 100.0 / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Users).
Why a scalar subquery for the denominator?
The denominator is the total user count, independent of the contest grouping, so a one-time scalar subquery is the cleanest source.
Any precision pitfall?
I multiply by 100.0 before dividing so it stays floating point, then ROUND to two decimals and order by percentage desc, contest_id asc.

followUpsQA.a: Total users is constant across contests, so compute it once.
To force float division and preserve decimals.
A user could register multiple rows; count them once per contest.

questions.q: Queries Quality and Percentage. Queries(query_name, result, position, rating). For each query_name compute quality = AVG(rating/position) and poor_query_percentage = percentage of queries with rating < 3, both rounded to 2 decimals.

answer.oneLiner: For each query name I need two metrics: quality is the average of the rating-to-position ratio across all rows for that query, and the poor query percentage is the fraction of rows where the rating is below 3. I compute both in a single GROUP BY query using the fact that a boolean comparison in MySQL evaluates to 1 or 0, so averaging it gives the fraction of true rows.

code.snippet:
SELECT query_name,
       ROUND(AVG(rating / position), 2) AS quality,
       ROUND(AVG(rating < 3) * 100, 2) AS poor_query_percentage
FROM Queries
WHERE query_name IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY query_name;
code.note: AVG(rating < 3) averages a boolean to get the fraction

keyPoints: Quality is computed as AVG of rating divided by position, which averages the per-row ratio of how good a result is relative to how high it ranked.
The poor query percentage uses AVG of the boolean expression rating less than 3, which equals 1 for poor queries and 0 for others, giving a fraction that when multiplied by 100 is the percentage.
Filtering out rows where query_name is NULL prevents a null group from appearing in the aggregated output.
Both metrics are wrapped in ROUND to two decimal places to satisfy the output format requirement.

dialogue.text: Per query_name, compute quality = AVG(rating/position) and the percent of rows with rating < 3.
I'd GROUP BY query_name, take AVG(rating / position) for quality, and AVG(rating < 3) * 100 for the poor percentage.
How does AVG(rating < 3) become a percentage?
rating < 3 evaluates to 1 or 0 per row, so its average is the fraction of poor queries; times 100 gives the percent.
Any rows to exclude?
I filter out NULL query_name, and round both metrics to two decimals.

followUpsQA.q: How does AVG(rating < 3) give a percentage?
followUpsQA.a: The boolean is 1/0, so the average times 100 is the percent.
NULL groups shouldn't appear in per-query metrics.
The average rating-to-position ratio per row.

questions.q: Nth Highest Salary. Employee(id PK, salary). Write a generic solution to return the Nth highest distinct salary (return NULL if it does not exist).

answer.oneLiner: For the N-th highest distinct salary, I apply DENSE_RANK to all salaries ordered descending and filter to the row where the rank equals N. DENSE_RANK assigns the same rank to tied salaries without skipping numbers, which ensures the N-th distinct salary is correctly identified even when multiple employees earn the same amount. If fewer than N distinct salaries exist, the query returns NULL.

code.note: DENSE_RANK dedupes ties; LIMIT/OFFSET works over a DISTINCT set

keyPoints: DENSE_RANK assigns the same rank number to equal salaries and does not leave gaps, so if two employees share the top salary they both receive rank 1 and the next distinct salary correctly gets rank 2.

Filtering to rows where the rank equals N gives the N-th distinct salary level, and wrapping the query in a MAX or using DISTINCT on the outer level collapses any ties at that rank into a single value.
Using LIMIT with OFFSET N minus 1 over DISTINCT salaries is an alternative in MySQL, and the DISTINCT is critical because duplicate salaries would shift the offset and return the wrong value.
When fewer than N distinct salaries exist in the table, no row matches the rank filter and the query returns NULL, which is the correct output.

dialogue.text: Write a generic solution for the N-th highest distinct salary, NULL if it doesn't exist.
I'd DENSE_RANK() salaries descending and pick rnk = N; DISTINCT salary in the outer select keeps ties collapsed.
What's the LIMIT/OFFSET alternative?
SELECT DISTINCT salary ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET N-1; the DISTINCT is essential so duplicates don't shift the offset.
What if there are fewer than N distinct salaries?
The query returns no row, which surfaces as NULL, exactly what's required.

followUpsQA.a: DENSE_RANK collapses tied salaries; ROW_NUMBER would not.
So duplicate salaries don't push the offset onto the wrong value.

followUpsQA.q: What happens when N exceeds the distinct count?
followUpsQA.a: No row, returned as NULL.

questions.q: Monthly Transactions I. Transactions(id, country, state ENUM('approved','declined'), amount, trans_date). For each month and country report total transaction count and amount, plus approved count and approved amount.

answer.oneLiner: The goal is to produce four metrics per month and country in a single query without making multiple passes through the data. I group by both the formatted month and country, compute the overall count and sum for all transactions, and then use conditional aggregation to compute the same metrics restricted to only the approved transactions.
keyPoints: DATE_FORMAT with the pattern percent-Y-percent-m groups rows by year and month together so January 2020 and January 2021 are treated as separate buckets.
COUNT of all rows gives the total transaction count and SUM of amount gives total revenue for all transactions in each month-country group.
SUM of the boolean expression state equal to approved evaluates to 1 for approved transactions and 0 otherwise, giving the count of approved transactions without a separate query.
SUM of IF state equals approved THEN amount ELSE 0 sums the amounts of only the approved transactions, which is the conditional revenue total.

code.snippet:
SELECT DATE_FORMAT(trans_date, '%Y-%m') AS month,
       country,
       COUNT(*)                                   AS trans_count,
       SUM(state = 'approved')                    AS approved_count,
       SUM(amount)                                AS trans_total_amount,
       SUM(IF(state = 'approved', amount, 0))     AS approved_total_amount
FROM Transactions
GROUP BY month, country;
code.note: Conditional aggregation avoids a second pass

dialogue.text: Per month and country, give total and approved transaction counts and amounts.
I group by the formatted month and country, then use conditional sums so approved metrics come from the same scan.
How do you count just the approved ones without a separate query?
SUM(state='approved') treats the boolean as 1/0, and SUM(IF(state='approved',amount,0)) totals only approved amounts.

followUpsQA.a: MONTH() drops the year, so different years would collide into the same bucket.
CASE WHEN state='approved' THEN amount ELSE 0 END works on any engine.

questions.q: Immediate Food Delivery II. Delivery(delivery_id PK, customer_id, order_date, customer_pref_delivery_date). An order is immediate when the preferred date equals the order date. Find the percentage of immediate orders among each customer's first order, rounded to 2 decimals.

answer.oneLiner: The approach is to first identify each customer's earliest order date using MIN grouped by customer, then filter the Delivery table to only those first-order rows, and finally compute the percentage that are immediate by averaging a boolean check. An order is immediate when the preferred delivery date matches the order date.
keyPoints: Each customer's first order is identified by finding the MIN of order_date per customer using GROUP BY.
The tuple IN subquery matches on both customer_id and the first order date together, so only the rows from each customer's earliest order day are retained.
AVG of the boolean expression order_date equal to customer_pref_delivery_date gives the fraction of first orders that are immediate, which multiplied by 100 is the percentage.

A window function alternative is to use ROW_NUMBER partitioned by customer ordered by order_date and keep row number 1, which gives more control when a customer has multiple orders on the same earliest date.

code.snippet:
SELECT ROUND(AVG(order_date = customer_pref_delivery_date) * 100, 2) AS immediate_percentage
FROM Delivery
WHERE (customer_id, order_date) IN (
    SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date)
    FROM Delivery
    GROUP BY customer_id
);
code.note: Filter to first orders, then average the boolean

dialogue.text: What share of customers' first orders are immediate?
I filter to each customer's MIN(order_date), then AVG the boolean order_date = preferred_date and multiply by 100.
Why average a boolean instead of COUNT/COUNT?
AVG of a 1/0 boolean is exactly the fraction of true rows, so it collapses the count-over-count into one expression.

followUpsQA.q: Could a window function replace the subquery?
followUpsQA.a: Yes — ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) and keep rn = 1.

followUpsQA.q: What if a customer has two orders on the same earliest date?
followUpsQA.a: The tuple-IN would keep both; the problem guarantees one first order, but ROW_NUMBER would force a single row.

questions.q: Game Play Analysis IV. Activity(player_id, device_id, event_date, games_played), PK (player_id, event_date). Report the fraction of players who logged in again the day immediately after their first login, rounded to 2 decimals.

answer.oneLiner: I find each player's first login date using MIN of event_date grouped by player, add one day to that date, and then check whether a row with that next-day date exists in the Activity table for the same player. The number of players who have that next-day row, divided by the total distinct player count, gives the day-one retention fraction.
keyPoints: DATE_ADD of the first login date plus an interval of 1 day computes the target return date, which is the day immediately after each player's debut.
The tuple IN on player_id and the computed next-day date checks whether the Activity table has an entry for that specific player on that specific date.

COUNT of DISTINCT player_id among the matching rows gives the number of players who returned the next day, and dividing by the total distinct player count from the whole table gives the retention fraction.
A window function alternative is to use LEAD of event_date over a partition ordered by date and filter to rows where the leading date is exactly one day after the current date.

code.snippet:
SELECT ROUND(
         COUNT(DISTINCT a.player_id) /
         (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT player_id) FROM Activity),
       2) AS fraction
FROM Activity a
WHERE (a.player_id, a.event_date) IN (
    SELECT player_id, DATE_ADD(MIN(event_date), INTERVAL 1 DAY)
    FROM Activity
    GROUP BY player_id
);
code.note: Match consecutive-day logins against first login + 1

dialogue.text: What fraction of players come back the day after their first login?
I compute each player's first date, then keep activity rows that fall on first_date + 1 day, and divide that count by total players.
Why the tuple (player_id, event_date) in the IN?
It ties the +1-day check to the same player, so a return login by a different player can't be miscounted.

followUpsQA.q: How would you generalize to N-day retention?
followUpsQA.a: Replace INTERVAL 1 DAY with INTERVAL N DAY in the match.
LEAD(event_date) OVER (PARTITION BY player_id ORDER BY event_date) and compare to first date + 1.

questions.q: Number of Unique Subjects Taught by Each Teacher. Teacher(teacher_id, subject_id, dept_id), PK (subject_id, dept_id). Count the distinct subjects each teacher teaches.

answer.oneLiner: Each teacher can teach the same subject in multiple departments, so to count the number of unique subjects a teacher covers I need COUNT of DISTINCT subject_id rather than a plain COUNT. The composite primary key allows the same subject to appear under different departments for the same teacher, which is why the deduplication is necessary.
keyPoints: COUNT of DISTINCT subject_id grouped by teacher_id counts the number of different subjects a teacher handles, treating the same subject in two departments as one subject.
The composite primary key on subject_id and dept_id allows a teacher to appear multiple times with the same subject_id if they teach that subject in more than one department.
If you forgot the DISTINCT and used plain COUNT, you would overcount teachers who teach the same subject in multiple departments.

code.note: COUNT(DISTINCT ...) is the whole trick

dialogue.text: How many unique subjects does each teacher teach?
Group by teacher_id and COUNT(DISTINCT subject_id), since one subject can appear under multiple departments.
What if you forgot DISTINCT?
You'd count subject/department rows, overstating teachers who teach the same subject in several departments.

followUpsQA.q: Why is plain COUNT(subject_id) wrong here?
followUpsQA.a: It counts rows, so a subject taught in two depts counts twice.
Yes — it's why duplicates by department exist, making DISTINCT necessary.

questions.q: User Activity for the Past 30 Days I. Activity(user_id, session_id, activity_date, activity_type). Find the daily active user count for the 30-day window ending 2019-07-27 inclusive (a user is active if they did anything that day).

answer.oneLiner: The 30-day window ending on July 27 2019 inclusive contains exactly 30 days, starting from June 28 2019. I filter to that range and then group by activity date, counting distinct user IDs so that a user who has multiple sessions on the same day is counted only once as active for that day.
keyPoints: The date range uses a half-open bound: activity_date greater than June 27 and less than or equal to July 27 captures exactly 30 days from June 28 through July 27 inclusive.
COUNT of DISTINCT user_id per date ensures a user who opened multiple sessions or performed multiple activities on the same day is counted only once as an active user for that day.
Days within the window that have no activity at all simply produce no row in the output, since there is no data to group on those dates.

code.snippet:
SELECT activity_date AS day,
       COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS active_users
FROM Activity
WHERE activity_date > '2019-06-27'
  AND activity_date <= '2019-07-27'
GROUP BY activity_date;
code.note: Half-open range gives exactly 30 days

dialogue.text: Daily active users for the 30 days ending 2019-07-27.
I bound activity_date to that 30-day window and group by date with COUNT(DISTINCT user_id).
Why DISTINCT?
A user can open several sessions a day; without DISTINCT they'd be counted multiple times as 'active'.

followUpsQA.a: Both express the same 30-day window; the strict form mirrors 'end minus 29 days'.

followUpsQA.q: How to also show zero-activity days?
followUpsQA.a: Left join against a generated calendar/date table.

questions.q: Product Sales Analysis III. Sales(sale_id, product_id, year, quantity, price), Product(product_id PK, product_name). For each product report the first year it sold, plus that year's quantity and price.

answer.oneLiner: To get the first year a product sold along with that year's quantity and price, I find the minimum year per product in a subquery and then use a tuple IN to match back to the actual rows from that year. This preserves all sales rows from the first year, which is important if a product had multiple transactions in its debut year.
keyPoints: MIN of year grouped by product_id identifies the earliest year each product appeared in the Sales table.
The tuple IN matching on both product_id and year returns every Sales row from that first year, not just a single row, so multiple transactions on the debut year are all included.
Grouping by product_id and taking MIN of the numeric columns would not work correctly here because MIN of quantity and MIN of price are computed independently and may not correspond to the same row.
A window function alternative is RANK partitioned by product_id ordered by year, keeping rows where the rank equals 1.

code.snippet:
SELECT s.product_id, s.year AS first_year, s.quantity, s.price
FROM Sales s
WHERE (s.product_id, s.year) IN (
    SELECT product_id, MIN(year)
    FROM Sales
    GROUP BY product_id
);
code.note: Tuple-IN keeps every row from the first selling year

dialogue.text: Give each product's first selling year with that year's quantity and price.
I find MIN(year) per product and keep rows matching the (product_id, year) tuple, which preserves multiple sales in that first year.
Why not just GROUP BY and MIN the columns?
MIN(quantity)/MIN(price) wouldn't be tied to the first year's actual row; the tuple match keeps the correct values.

followUpsQA.a: RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY product_id ORDER BY year) then filter rank = 1.
RANK keeps all first-year rows; ROW_NUMBER would arbitrarily keep one.

questions.q: Classes More Than 5 Students. Courses(student, class), PK (student, class). List classes that have at least 5 students.

answer.oneLiner: This is a classic GROUP BY and HAVING problem. I group by class name and count the students in each group, then use HAVING to keep only classes with a count of at least 5. HAVING is what allows you to filter on an aggregated value like a count, because WHERE runs before the grouping happens and has no access to those aggregates yet.
keyPoints: GROUP BY class produces one row per class with all the student rows for that class collapsed together.
COUNT of student within each group gives the number of students enrolled in that class.
HAVING is what you use when you want to filter on an aggregated value like a count or sum, because WHERE runs before the grouping happens and does not have access to those aggregates yet.
Because the composite primary key ensures each student-class combination is unique, plain COUNT of student is equivalent to COUNT of DISTINCT student here.

code.snippet:
SELECT class
FROM Courses
GROUP BY class
HAVING COUNT(student) >= 5;
code.note: HAVING for post-aggregation filtering

dialogue.text: Which classes have 5 or more students?
Group by class and keep those with COUNT(student) >= 5 via HAVING.
Why HAVING and not WHERE?
WHERE filters individual rows before grouping; the count only exists after aggregation, so HAVING is required.

followUpsQA.a: Not here — the composite PK already makes each (student,class) unique.
WHERE is pre-aggregation per row; HAVING is post-aggregation per group.

questions.q: Find Followers Count. Followers(user_id, follower_id), PK (user_id, follower_id). For each user output the number of followers, ordered by user_id.

answer.oneLiner: This is a straightforward aggregation: group the Followers table by user_id and count the rows in each group to get the number of followers per user. The composite primary key on user_id and follower_id guarantees there are no duplicate follower entries, so plain COUNT is safe without needing DISTINCT.
keyPoints: GROUP BY user_id groups all follower rows for each user together so COUNT can tally them.

The composite primary key on user_id and follower_id prevents any user from having the same follower recorded more than once, which means COUNT of follower_id gives the exact follower count without risk of overcounting.
ORDER BY user_id at the end sorts the output by user as required.

code.snippet:
SELECT user_id, COUNT(follower_id) AS followers_count
FROM Followers
GROUP BY user_id
ORDER BY user_id;
code.note: Straight COUNT per group

dialogue.text: Count followers per user, sorted by user_id.
Group by user_id with COUNT(follower_id) and ORDER BY user_id.
Any risk of double counting?
No — the (user_id, follower_id) primary key prevents duplicate follower rows.

followUpsQA.a: Only if duplicates were allowed; the PK rules that out here.
They won't appear; you'd need a left join from a Users table to include them.

questions.q: Biggest Single Number. MyNumbers(num) — may contain duplicates. Return the largest number that appears exactly once, or NULL if none.

answer.oneLiner: I group the numbers by value and use HAVING to keep only those that appear exactly once, giving me the set of singleton values. Then I wrap that in an outer MAX to return the largest singleton, and the nice property of MAX is that it returns NULL when applied to an empty set, which handles the edge case where no singletons exist without any extra conditional logic.
keyPoints: GROUP BY num with HAVING COUNT of all rows equal to 1 isolates values that appear exactly once in the table.
Wrapping the grouped result in an outer MAX returns the largest qualifying value.
MAX over an empty result set returns NULL by definition in SQL, so the edge case where no number appears exactly once is handled automatically without needing a CASE expression or IFNULL.

code.snippet:
SELECT MAX(num) AS num
FROM (
    SELECT num
    FROM MyNumbers
    GROUP BY num
    HAVING COUNT(*) = 1
) t;
code.note: MAX of empty set => NULL, satisfying the edge case

dialogue.text: Largest number appearing exactly once, else NULL.
I group by num, keep those with COUNT = 1, then MAX the result; the MAX wrapper gives NULL when none qualify.
Why does the NULL case work without extra handling?
Aggregating MAX over zero rows returns NULL by definition, which is exactly the required output.

followUpsQA.q: Why the subquery instead of HAVING + ORDER BY LIMIT 1?
followUpsQA.a: ORDER BY LIMIT 1 returns no row (not NULL) when empty; MAX guarantees a NULL row.

followUpsQA.q: Could you use a window function?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY num), but it's heavier than GROUP BY here.

questions.q: Customers Who Bought All Products. Customer(customer_id, product_key), Product(product_key PK). Find customers who bought every product.

answer.oneLiner: To find customers who bought every product, I count the distinct products each customer purchased and compare that number to the total number of products in the Product table. A customer who has bought all products will have a distinct product count equal to the catalog size.
keyPoints: COUNT of DISTINCT product_key per customer counts how many different products that customer has purchased, deduplicating in case they bought the same product more than once.
A scalar subquery computing COUNT of all rows from the Product table gives the total number of products in the catalog.
HAVING compares the two counts and keeps only customers whose distinct product count matches the total, which is the relational division pattern.
